text.KJV: Fulfill her weeke, and wee will giue thee this also, for the seruice which thou shalt serue with mee, yet seuen other yeeres.
text.NABRE: Finish the bridal week for this one, and then the other will also be given to you in return for another seven years of service with me.”
text.DRB: Make up the week of days of this match: and I will give thee this same also, for the work that thou shalt serve me other seven years.

text.NRSV: Jacob did so, and completed her week; then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as a wife.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob did so, and fulfilled the week of this one,—and he gave him Rachel his daughter—to him to wife.
text.KJV: And Iacob did so, and fulfilled her weeke: and he gaue him Rachel his daughter to wife also.
text.NABRE: Jacob did so. He finished the bridal week for the one, and then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as a wife.
text.DRB: He yielded to his pleasure; and after the week was past, he married Rachel to wife:

text.NRSV: (Laban gave his maid Bilhah to his daughter Rachel to be her maid.)
text.Rotherham: And Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid,—to be hers as a handmaid.
text.KJV: And Laban gaue to Rachel his daughter, Bilhah his handmayd, to be her mayd.
text.NABRE: Laban assigned his maidservant Bilhah to his daughter Rachel as her maidservant.
text.DRB: to whom her father had delivered Bala for to be her servant.

text.NRSV: So Jacob went in to Rachel also, and he loved Rachel more than Leah. He served Laban for another seven years.
text.Rotherham: So he went in, unto Rachel also, and loved, Rachel also, more than Leah,—and he served with him, yet seven years more.
text.KJV: And hee went in also vnto Rachel, and he loued also Rachel more then Leah, and serued with him yet seuen other yeeres.
text.NABRE: Jacob then consummated his marriage with Rachel also, and he loved her more than Leah. Thus he served Laban another seven years. Jacob’s Children.
text.DRB: And having at length obtained the marriage that he wished, he preferred the love of the later before the former, serving with him other seven years.

text.NRSV: When the Lord saw that Leah was unloved, he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren.
text.Rotherham: And, when Yahweh saw that Leah was hated, he granted her to bear children,—whereas, Rachel, was barren.
text.KJV: ¶ And when the LORD saw that Leah was hated, hee opened her wombe: but Rachel was barren.
text.NABRE: When the Lord saw that Leah was unloved, he made her fruitful, while Rachel was barren.
text.DRB: And our Lord seeing that he despised Lia, opened her womb, her sister remaining barren.

text.NRSV: Leah conceived and bore a son, and she named him Reuben; for she said, “Because the Lord has looked on my affliction; surely now my husband will love me.”
text.Rotherham: So Leah conceived, and bare a son, and called his name Reuben,—for she said, Because, Yahweh hath looked upon my humiliation, For, now, will my husband love me.
text.KJV: And Leah conceiued and bare a sonne, and shee called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely, the LORD hath looked vpon my affliction; now therefore my husband will loue me.
text.NABRE: Leah conceived and bore a son, and she named him Reuben; for she said, “It means, ‘The Lord saw my misery; surely now my husband will love me.’”
text.DRB: Who conceived and bare a son, and called his name Ruben, saying: Our Lord saw mine affliction: now my husband will love me.

text.NRSV: She conceived again and bore a son, and said, “Because the Lord has heard that I am hated, he has given me this son also”; and she named him Simeon.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again, and bare a son, and said. Because Yahweh heard that, I, was hated, he gave me, this one also. So she called his name Simeon.
text.KJV: And shee conceiued againe, and bare a sonne, and saide, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, hee hath therefore giuen mee this sonne also, and she called his name Simeon.
text.NABRE: She conceived again and bore a son, and said, “It means, ‘The Lord heard that I was unloved,’ and therefore he has given me this one also”; so she named him Simeon.
text.DRB: And again she conceived and bare a son, and said: For because our Lord heard that I was contemned, he hath given this also to me: and she called his name Simeon.

text.NRSV: Again she conceived and bore a son, and said, “Now this time my husband will be joined to me, because I have borne him three sons”; therefore he was named Levi.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again and bare a son, and said, Now—this time, will my husband be bound unto me, For I have borne him three sons,—For this cause, called she his name Levi.
text.KJV: And shee conceiued againe, and bare a sonne, and said, Now this time will my husband be ioyned vnto me, because I haue borne him three sonnes: therefore was his name called Leui.
text.NABRE: Again she conceived and bore a son, and she said, “Now at last my husband will become attached to me, since I have now borne him three sons”; that is why she named him Levi.
text.DRB: And she conceived the third time, and bare another son: and said: Now also my husband will be joined to me, for because I have born him three sons: and therefore she called his name, Levi.

text.NRSV: She conceived again and bore a son, and said, “This time I will praise the Lord”; therefore she named him Judah; then she ceased bearing.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again and bare a son, and said This time, will I praise Yahweh, For which cause, she called his name Judah. And she left off bearing.
text.KJV: And shee conceiued againe, and bare a sonne: and she said, Now wil I praise the LORD: therefore she called his name Iudah, and left bearing.
text.NABRE: Once more she conceived and bore a son, and she said, “This time I will give thanks to the Lord”; therefore she named him Judah. Then she stopped bearing children.
text.DRB: The fourth time she conceived and bare a son, and said: Now will I confess to our Lord. And for this she called him Juda: And she left bearing.

text.NRSV: When Rachel saw that she bore Jacob no children, she envied her sister; and she said to Jacob, “Give me children, or I shall die!”
text.Rotherham: And Rachel saw she had borne no children unto Jacob, so Rachel became envious of her sister,—and said unto Jacob, Come! give me children, or else, I die.
text.KJV: And when Rachel saw that shee bare Iacob no children, Rachel enuied her sister, and said vnto Iacob, Giue mee children, or els I die.
text.NABRE: When Rachel saw that she had not borne children to Jacob, she became envious of her sister. She said to Jacob, “Give me children or I shall die!”
text.DRB: And Rachel seeing she was unfruitful, envied her sister, and said to her husband: Give me children, otherwise I shall die.

text.NRSV: Jacob became very angry with Rachel and said, “Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?”
text.Rotherham: Then kindled the anger of Jacob with Rachel,—and he said, Am I, in the place of God, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?
text.KJV: And Iacobs anger was kindled against Rachel, and he said, Am I in Gods stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the wombe?
text.NABRE: Jacob became angry with Rachel and said, “Can I take the place of God, who has denied you the fruit of the womb?”
text.DRB: With whom Jacob being angry answered: Am I as God, who hath deprived thee of the fruit of thy womb?

text.NRSV: Then she said, “Here is my maid Bilhah; go in to her, that she may bear upon my knees and that I too may have children through her.”
text.Rotherham: And she said, Lo! my handmaid, Bilhah. Go in unto her,—that she may bear upon my knees, so that, I also, may be builded up by her!
text.KJV: And she said, Behold my mayde Bilhah: goe in vnto her, and she shall beare vpon my knees, that I may also have children by her.
text.NABRE: She replied, “Here is my maidservant Bilhah. Have intercourse with her, and let her give birth on my knees, so that I too may have children through her.”
text.DRB: But she said: I have here my servant Bala: Company with her, that she may bear upon my knees, and I may have children of her.

text.NRSV: So she gave him her maid Bilhah as a wife; and Jacob went in to her.
text.Rotherham: And she gave him Bilhah her handmaid, to wife,—and Jacob went in unto her;
text.KJV: And shee gaue him Bilhah her handmayd to wife: and Iacob went in vnto her.
text.NABRE: So she gave him her maidservant Bilhah as wife, and Jacob had intercourse with her.
text.DRB: And she gave him Bala unto marriage: who,

text.NRSV: And Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son.
text.Rotherham: and Bilhah conceived, and bare unto Jacob a son;
text.KJV: And Bilhah conceiued and bare Iacob a sonne.
text.NABRE: When Bilhah conceived and bore a son for Jacob,
text.DRB: When her husband had companied with her, conceived and bare a son.

text.NRSV: Then Rachel said, “God has judged me, and has also heard my voice and given me a son”; therefore she named him Dan.
text.Rotherham: and Rachel said God hath vindicated me. Moreover also he hath hearkened unto my voice, and hath given me a son. For this cause, called she his name Dan.
text.KJV: And Rachel said, God hath iudged me, and hath also heard my voyce, and hath giuen me a sonne; therefore called she his name Dan.
text.NABRE: Rachel said, “God has vindicated me; indeed he has heeded my plea and given me a son.” Therefore she named him Dan.
text.DRB: And Rachel said: Our Lord hath judged for me and hath heard my voice, giving me a son, and therefore she called his name, Dan.

text.NRSV: Rachel’s maid Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.
text.Rotherham: And Bilhah, Rachel’s handmaid conceived again, and bare a second son to Jacob.
text.KJV: And Bilhah Rachels mayd conceiued againe, and bare Iacob a second sonne.
text.NABRE: Rachel’s maidservant Bilhah conceived again and bore a second son for Jacob,
text.DRB: And again Bala conceiving bare another,

text.NRSV: Then Rachel said, “With mighty wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and have prevailed”; so she named him Naphtali.
text.Rotherham: Then said Rachel With wrestlings of God, have I wrestled with my sister Yea I have prevailed. So she called his name, Naphtali.
text.KJV: And Rachel saide, With great wrastlings haue I wrastled with my sister, and I haue preuailed: and she called his name Naphtali.
text.NABRE: and Rachel said, “I have wrestled strenuously with my sister, and I have prevailed.” So she named him Naphtali.
text.DRB: for whom Rachel said: God hath compared me with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called him Nephthali.

text.NRSV: When Leah saw that she had ceased bearing children, she took her maid Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife.
text.Rotherham: Then saw Leah, that she had left off bearing,—so she took Zilpah, her handmaid, and gave her to Jacob to wife.
text.KJV: When Leah saw that she had left bearing, shee tooke Zilpah her mayde, and gaue her Iacob to wife.
text.NABRE: When Leah saw that she had ceased to bear children, she took her maidservant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as wife.
text.DRB: Lia perceiving that she had left bearing, delivered Zelpha her handmaid to her husband.

text.NRSV: Then Leah’s maid Zilpah bore Jacob a son.
text.Rotherham: And Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid, bare to Jacob a son:
text.KJV: And Zilpah Leahs mayde bare Iacob a sonne.
text.NABRE: So Leah’s maidservant Zilpah bore a son for Jacob.
text.DRB: Who conceiving and bringing forth a son,

text.NRSV: And Leah said, “Good fortune!” so she named him Gad.
text.Rotherham: and Leah said, Victory hath come! So she called his name Gad.
text.KJV: And Leah said, A troupe commeth: and she called his name Gad.
text.NABRE: Leah then said, “What good luck!” So she named him Gad.
text.DRB: she said: Happily. And therefore called his name Gad.

text.NRSV: Leah’s maid Zilpah bore Jacob a second son.
text.Rotherham: And Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid bare a second son to Jacob;
text.KJV: And Zilpah Leahs mayde bare Iacob a second sonne.
text.NABRE: Then Leah’s maidservant Zilpah bore a second son to Jacob;
text.DRB: Zelpha also bare another.

text.NRSV: And Leah said, “Happy am I! For the women will call me happy”; so she named him Asher.
text.Rotherham: and Leah said, For my happiness, surely happy have daughters pronounced me. So she called his name, Asher.
text.KJV: And Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher.
text.NABRE: and Leah said, “What good fortune, because women will call me fortunate!” So she named him Asher.
text.DRB: And Lia said: This is for my blessedness: for women will call me blessed. Therefore she called him Aser.

text.NRSV: In the days of wheat harvest Reuben went and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them to his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, “Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes.”
text.Rotherham: Then went Reuben, in the days of the wheat harvest, and found mandrakes, in the field, and brought them in unto Leah his mother, and Rachel said unto Leah, Pray give me, some of the mandrakes of thy son
text.KJV: ¶ And Reuben went in the dayes of wheat haruest, & found Mandrakes in the field, and brought them vnto his mother Leah. Then Rachel saide to Leah, Giue me, I pray thee, of thy sonnes Mandrakes.
text.NABRE: One day, during the wheat harvest, Reuben went out and came upon some mandrakes in the field which he brought home to his mother Leah. Rachel said to Leah, “Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes.”
text.DRB: And Ruben, going forth in the time of wheat harvest into the field, found mandragoras: which he brought to his mother Lia. And Rachel said: Give me part of thy son's mandragoras.

text.NRSV: But she said to her, “Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband? Would you take away my son’s mandrakes also?” Rachel said, “Then he may lie with you tonight for your son’s mandrakes.”
text.Rotherham: And she said to her, Is it, a small thing, that thou hast taken away my husband? And wouldst thou take away, even the mandrakes of my son? Then said Rachel, Therefore, shall he lie with thee to-night, for the mandrakes of thy son.
text.KJV: And shee said vnto her, Is it a small matter, that thou hast taken my husband? and wouldst thou take away my sonnes Mandrakes also? and Rachel said, Therefore hee shall lye with thee to night, for thy sonnes Mandrakes.
text.NABRE: Leah replied, “Was it not enough for you to take away my husband, that you must now take my son’s mandrakes too?” Rachel answered, “In that case Jacob may lie with you tonight in exchange for your son’s mandrakes.”
text.DRB: She answered: Dost thou think it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband from me, unless thou take also my son's mandragoras? Rachel said: For thy son's mandragoras let him sleep with thee this night.

text.NRSV: When Jacob came from the field in the evening, Leah went out to meet him, and said, “You must come in to me; for I have hired you with my son’s mandrakes.” So he lay with her that night.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob came in from the field, in the evening, so Leah went out to meet him and said: Unto me, shalt thou come in, for I have hired, thee, even with the mandrakes of my son. And he lay with her that night,
text.KJV: And Iacob came out of the field in the euening, and Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in vnto mee: for surely I haue hired thee with my sonnes Mandrakes. And hee lay with her that night.
text.NABRE: That evening, when Jacob came in from the field, Leah went out to meet him. She said, “You must have intercourse with me, because I have hired you with my son’s mandrakes.” So that night he lay with her,
text.DRB: And when Jacob returned at even from the field, Lia went out to meet him, and said: Company with me, because with wages I have hired thee for my son's mandragoras. And he slept with her that night.

text.NRSV: And God heeded Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.
text.Rotherham: And God hearkened unto Leah,—and she conceived and bare to Jacob a fifth son.
text.KJV: And God hearkened vnto Leah, and she conceiued, and bare Iacob the fift sonne.
text.NABRE: and God listened to Leah; she conceived and bore a fifth son to Jacob.
text.DRB: And God heard her prayers: and she conceived and bare the fifth son,

text.NRSV: Leah said, “God has given me my hire because I gave my maid to my husband”; so she named him Issachar.
text.Rotherham: Then said Leah. God hath given my hire, in that I gave my handmaid to my husband. So she called his name, Issachar.
text.KJV: And Leah said, God hath giuen mee my hire, because I haue giuen my mayden to my husband: and she called his name Issachar.
text.NABRE: Leah then said, “God has given me my wages for giving my maidservant to my husband”; so she named him Issachar.
text.DRB: And said: God hath given me a reward, because I gave my handmaid to my husband. And she called his name Issachar.

text.NRSV: And Leah conceived again, and she bore Jacob a sixth son.
text.Rotherham: And Leah conceived again and bare a sixth son to Jacob.
text.KJV: And Leah conceiued againe, and bare Iacob the sixth sonne.
text.NABRE: Leah conceived again and bore a sixth son to Jacob;
text.DRB: Again Lia conceiving, bare the sixth son,

text.NRSV: Then Leah said, “God has endowed me with a good dowry; now my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons”; so she named him Zebulun.
text.Rotherham: Then said Leah, God hath dowered me even me with a hand-some dowry, Now! will my husband dwell with me, for I have borne him six sons. So she called his name, Zebulon.
text.KJV: And Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry: Now will my husband dwel with me, because I haue borne him sixe sonnes: and shee called his name Zebulun.
text.NABRE: and Leah said, “God has brought me a precious gift. This time my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons”; so she named him Zebulun.
text.DRB: & said: God hath endowed me with a good dowry: this turn also my husband will be with me, for because I have born him six sons, and therefore she called his name, Zabulon.

text.NRSV: Afterwards she bore a daughter, and named her Dinah.
text.Rotherham: And afterwards she bare a daughter,—so she called her name, Dinah.
text.KJV: And afterwardes shee bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah.
text.NABRE: Afterwards she gave birth to a daughter, and she named her Dinah.
text.DRB: After whom she bare a daughter named Dina.

text.NRSV: Then God remembered Rachel, and God heeded her and opened her womb.
text.Rotherham: Then God remembered Rachel,—and God hearkened unto her, and granted her to bear.
text.KJV: ¶ And God remembred Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her wombe.
text.NABRE: Then God remembered Rachel. God listened to her and made her fruitful.
text.DRB: Our Lord also remembering Rachel, heard her, and opened her womb.

text.NRSV: She conceived and bore a son, and said, “God has taken away my reproach”;
text.Rotherham: So she conceived and bare a son,—and said, God hath taken away my reproach;
text.KJV: And shee conceiued and bare a sonne, and said; God hath taken away my reproch:
text.NABRE: She conceived and bore a son, and she said, “God has removed my disgrace.”
text.DRB: Who conceived and bare a son, saying: God hath taken away my reproach.

text.NRSV: and she named him Joseph, saying, “May the Lord add to me another son!”
text.Rotherham: So she called his name Joseph, saying, Yahweh is adding unto me another son.
text.KJV: And shee called his name Ioseph, and saide, The LORD shall adde to me another sonne.
text.NABRE: She named him Joseph, saying, “May the Lord add another son for me!” Jacob Outwits Laban.
text.DRB: And she called his name, Joseph, saying: Our Lord add to me another son.

text.NRSV: When Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, “Send me away, that I may go to my own home and country.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when Rachel had borne Joseph, that Jacob said unto Laban, Let me go that I may take my journey, unto my place, and to my land.
text.KJV: ¶ And it came to passe when Rachel had borne Ioseph, that Iacob said vnto Laban, Send me away, that I may goe vnto mine owne place, and to my countrey.
text.NABRE: After Rachel gave birth to Joseph, Jacob said to Laban: “Allow me to go to my own region and land.
text.DRB: And when Joseph was born, Jacob said to his father in law: Dismiss me that I may return into my country, and to my land.

text.NRSV: Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and let me go; for you know very well the service I have given you.”
text.Rotherham: Come, give me my wives and my children for whom I have served thee and let me take my journey,—for, thou, knowest my service wherewith I have served thee.
text.KJV: Giue mee my wiues and my children, for whom I haue serued thee, and let me goe: for thou knowest my seruice which I haue done thee.
text.NABRE: Give me my wives and my children for whom I served you and let me go, for you know the service that I rendered you.”
text.DRB: Give me my wives, and my children, for whom I have served thee, that I may depart: thou knowest the service that I have served thee.

text.NRSV: But Laban said to him, “If you will allow me to say so, I have learned by divination that the Lord has blessed me because of you;
text.Rotherham: And Laban said unto him, If, I pray thee, I have found favour in thine eyes, …I have divined that Yahweh hath blessed me for thy sake.
text.KJV: And Laban said vnto him, I pray thee, if I haue found fauour in thine eyes, tary: for I haue learned by experience, that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake.
text.NABRE: Laban answered him: “If you will please! I have learned through divination that the Lord has blessed me because of you.”
text.DRB: Laban said to him: Let me find grace in thy sight: I have learned by experience that God hath blessed me for thy sake:

text.NRSV: name your wages, and I will give it.”
text.Rotherham: And he said,—Come, fix thy wages for me, that I may give it!
text.KJV: And he said, Appoint me thy wages, and I will giue it.
text.NABRE: He continued, “State the wages I owe you, and I will pay them.”
text.DRB: appoint thee wages which I shall give thee.

text.NRSV: Jacob said to him, “You yourself know how I have served you, and how your cattle have fared with me.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him, Thou, knowest how I have served thee,—and how thy cattle have fared, with me.
text.KJV: And hee said vnto him, Thou knowest how I haue serued thee, and how thy cattell was with me.
text.NABRE: Jacob replied: “You know what work I did for you and how well your livestock fared under my care;
text.DRB: But he answered: Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how great thy possession hath been in my hands.

text.NRSV: For you had little before I came, and it has increased abundantly; and the Lord has blessed you wherever I turned. But now when shall I provide for my own household also?”
text.Rotherham: For it was, little, that thou hadst—before I came and then it brake forth into multitude, and Yahweh blessed thee at my every step. Now, therefore, when am, I, also to do something for my own house?
text.KJV: For it was little which thou hadst before I came; and it is now increased vnto a multitude; and the LORD hath blessed thee since my comming: and now when shall I prouide for mine owne house also?
text.NABRE: the little you had before I came has grown into an abundance, since the Lord has blessed you in my company. Now, when can I do something for my own household as well?”
text.DRB: Thou hadest a small thing before I came to thee, and now thou art made rich: and our Lord hath blessed thee at my coming in. It is reason therefore that once I provide also for mine own house.

text.NRSV: He said, “What shall I give you?” Jacob said, “You shall not give me anything; if you will do this for me, I will again feed your flock and keep it:
text.Rotherham: And he said, What shall I give thee? And Jacob said: Thou shall give me, nothing at all, If thou wilt do for me this thing, I will return, I will shepherd thy flock I will keep it:
text.KJV: And hee said, what shall I giue thee? and Iacob said, Thou shalt not giue me any thing; if thou wilt doe this thing for mee, I will againe feed and keepe thy flocke.
text.NABRE: Laban asked, “What should I give you?” Jacob answered: “You do not have to give me anything. If you do this thing for me, I will again pasture and tend your sheep.
text.DRB: And Laban said: What shall I give thee? But he said: I will nothing: but if thou wilt do that which I demand, I will feed and keep thy sheep again.

text.NRSV: let me pass through all your flock today, removing from it every speckled and spotted sheep and every black lamb, and the spotted and speckled among the goats; and such shall be my wages.
text.Rotherham: Let me pass through all thy flock to-day, removing from thence every young one that is speckled and spotted and every young one that is dark-coloured among the young sheep, and spotted and speckled among the goats, and they shall be my wages.
text.KJV: I will passe through all thy flocke to day, remoouing from thence all the speckled and spotted cattell: and all the browne cattell among the sheepe, and the spotted and speckled among the goates, and of such shalbe my hire.
text.NABRE: Let me go through your whole flock today and remove from it every dark animal among the lambs and every spotted or speckled one among the goats. These will be my wages.
text.DRB: Go round about all the flocks, and separate all the sheep of diverse colours, of speckled fleece: and what-soever shall be russet and spotted, and of diverse colours, as well in the sheep as in the goats, shall be my wages.

text.NRSV: So my honesty will answer for me later, when you come to look into my wages with you. Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and black among the lambs, if found with me, shall be counted stolen.”
text.Rotherham: And my righteousness shall answer for me on a future day, when thou shall come in respecting my hire that is before thee, Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and dark-coloured among the young sheep, stolen, shall it be accounted, if found with me.
text.KJV: So shall my righteousnesse answere for mee in time to come, when it shall come for my hire, before thy face: euery one that is not speckled and spotted amongst the goates, and browne amongst the sheepe, that shalbe counted stollen with me.
text.NABRE: In the future, whenever you check on my wages, my honesty will testify for me: any animal that is not speckled or spotted among the goats, or dark among the lambs, got into my possession by theft!”
text.DRB: And my justice shall answer for me tomorrow before thee, when the time of the bargain shall come: and all that shall not be of diverse colours, and spotted, and russet, as well in the sheep as in the goats, shall accuse me of theft.

text.NRSV: Laban said, “Good! Let it be as you have said.”
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban, Lo! would it might be according to thy word!
text.KJV: And Laban saide, Beholde, I would it might bee according to thy word.
text.NABRE: Laban said, “Very well. Let it be as you say.”
text.DRB: And Laban said: I like well that thou demandest.

text.NRSV: But that day Laban removed the male goats that were striped and spotted, and all the female goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white on it, and every lamb that was black, and put them in charge of his sons;
text.Rotherham: So he removed, on that day, the he-goats that were striped and spotted and all the she-goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white in it, and every dark-coloured one among the young sheep,—and delivered them into the hand of his sons;
text.KJV: And he remoued that day the hee goates that were ring-straked, and spotted, and all the shee goats that were speckled and spotted, and euery one that had some white in it, and all the browne amongst the sheepe, and gaue them into the hand of his sonnes.
text.NABRE: That same day Laban removed the streaked and spotted he-goats and all the speckled and spotted she-goats, all those with some white on them, as well as every dark lamb, and he put them in the care of his sons.
text.DRB: And he separated the same day the she goats, and the sheep, and the he goats, and the rams of diverse colours, and spotted: and all the flock of one colour, that is of white and black fleece, he delivered in the hand of his sons.

text.NRSV: and he set a distance of three days’ journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob was pasturing the rest of Laban’s flock.
text.Rotherham: then put he a journey of three days between himself and Jacob,—but, Jacob himself, continued tending the flocks of Laban that were left.
text.KJV: And hee set three dayes iourney betwixt himselfe and Iacob: and Iacob fed the rest of Labans flocks.
text.NABRE: Then he put a three days’ journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob was pasturing the rest of Laban’s flock.
text.DRB: And he put a space of three days' journey betwixt him and his son in law, who fed the rest of his flock.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane, and peeled white streaks in them, exposing the white of the rods.
text.Rotherham: So then Jacob took him rods of young storax, and hazel and maple,—and peeled in them white stripes, laying bare the white which was on the rods.
text.KJV: ¶ And Iacob tooke him rods of greene poplar, and of the hasel and chesnut tree, and pilled white strakes in them, and made the white appeare which was in the rods.
text.NABRE: Jacob, however, got some fresh shoots of poplar, almond and plane trees, and he peeled white stripes in them by laying bare the white core of the shoots.
text.DRB: Jacob therefore taking green rods of the poplar, and of the almond, and of the plane-trees, in part pilled them: and when the barks were taken off, in the parts that were pilled there appeared whiteness: but the parts that were whole remained green: and by this means the colour was made diverse.

text.NRSV: He set the rods that he had peeled in front of the flocks in the troughs, that is, the watering places, where the flocks came to drink. And since they bred when they came to drink,
text.Rotherham: And he set the rods which he had peeled, in the channels in the troughs of water,—where the flocks came in to drink straight before the flocks, and the females of the flock used to be in heat when they came in to drink;
text.KJV: And he set the rods which he had pilled, before the flockes in the gutters in the watering troughes when the flocks came to drinke, that they should conceiue when they came to drinke.
text.NABRE: The shoots that he had peeled he then set upright in the watering troughs where the animals came to drink, so that they would be in front of them. When the animals were in heat as they came to drink,
text.DRB: And he put them in the troughs, where the water was poured out: that when the flocks should come to drink, they might have the rods before their eyes, and in the sight of them conceive.

text.NRSV: the flocks bred in front of the rods, and so the flocks produced young that were striped, speckled, and spotted.
text.Rotherham: and the males of the flock were in heat before the rods,—so the flocks brought forth ring-straked, speckled, and spotted.
text.KJV: And the flockes conceiued before the rods, and brought forth cattell ringstraked, speckled and spotted.
text.NABRE: the goats mated by the shoots, and so they gave birth to streaked, speckled and spotted young.
text.DRB: And it came to pass that in the very heat of the ramming, the sheep beheld the rods, and brought forth spotted, and of diverse colours, and speckled.

text.NRSV: Jacob separated the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the striped and the completely black animals in the flock of Laban; and he put his own droves apart, and did not put them with Laban’s flock.
text.Rotherham: The rams also, did Jacob separate, and then set the faces of the flocks towards the ring-straked and all the dark-coloured, among the flocks of Laban,—and he put his own droves by themselves, and put them not with the flocks of Laban.
text.KJV: And Iacob did separate the lambes, and set the faces of the flockes toward the ring-straked, and all the browne in the flocke of Laban: and he put his owne flocks by themselues, and put them not vnto Labans cattell.
text.NABRE: The sheep, on the other hand, Jacob kept apart, and he made these animals face the streaked or completely dark animals of Laban. Thus he produced flocks of his own, which he did not put with Laban’s flock.
text.DRB: And Jacob divided the flock, and put the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the rams: and all the white and the black were Laban's, and the rest, Jacob's, when the flocks were separated one from the other.

text.NRSV: Whenever the stronger of the flock were breeding, Jacob laid the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the flock, that they might breed among the rods,
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass whensoever the stronger of the flocks were in heat, that Jacob set the rods before the eyes of the flocks in the channels,—that the females might be in heat among the rods;
text.KJV: And it came to passe whensoeuer the stronger cattell did conceiue, that Iacob layd the rods before the eyes of the cattell in the gutters, that they might conceiue among the rods.
text.NABRE: Whenever the hardier animals were in heat, Jacob would set the shoots in the troughs in full view of these animals, so that they mated by the shoots;
text.DRB: Therefore when the ewes went to ram, in the prime time Jacob put the rods in the troughs of water before the eyes of the rams, and of the ewes, that in looking upon them they might conceive:

text.NRSV: but for the feebler of the flock he did not lay them there; so the feebler were Laban’s, and the stronger Jacob’s.
text.Rotherham: but, when the flocks were late in bearing, he did not set them,—and so the late-born were Laban’s, but the strong ones Jacob’s.
text.KJV: But when the cattel were feeble, hee put them not in: so the feebler were Labans, and the stronger Iacobs.
text.NABRE: but with the weaker animals he would not put the shoots there. So the feeble animals would go to Laban, but the hardy ones to Jacob.
text.DRB: but when the later coming was, and the last conceiving, he did not put them. And those that were lateward became Laban's: and they of the prime time, Jacob's.

text.NRSV: Thus the man grew exceedingly rich, and had large flocks, and male and female slaves, and camels and donkeys.
text.Rotherham: Thus did the man break forth, exceedingly,—thus came he to have flocks in abundance, and maid-servants, and men-servants and camels and asses.
text.KJV: And the man increased exceedingly, and had much cattell, and maydseruants, and men seruants, and camels, and asses.
text.NABRE: So the man grew exceedingly prosperous, and he owned large flocks, male and female servants, camels, and donkeys.
text.DRB: And the man was enriched beyond measure, and he had many flock, women servants and men servants, camels and asses.

text.NRSV: Now Jacob heard that the sons of Laban were saying, “Jacob has taken all that was our father’s; he has gained all this wealth from what belonged to our father.”
text.Rotherham: Then heard he the words of the sons of Laban, saying, Jacob hath taken away all that belonged to our father; Yea out of what belonged to our father, hath he made all this wealth.
text.KJV: And he heard the words of Labans sonnes, saying, Iacob hath taken away all that was our fathers; and of that which was of our fathers, hath hee gotten all this glory.
text.NABRE: Jacob heard that Laban’s sons were saying, “Jacob has taken everything that belonged to our father, and he has produced all this wealth from our father’s property.”
text.DRB: After he heard the words of Laban's sons saying: Jacob hath taken all that was our father's, and being enriched of his substance, is become great:

text.NRSV: And Jacob saw that Laban did not regard him as favorably as he did before.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob looked upon the face of Laban,—and lo! it was not with him as afore-time.
text.KJV: And Iacob behelde the countenance of Laban, and behold, it was not toward him as before.
text.NABRE: Jacob perceived, too, that Laban’s attitude toward him was not what it had previously been.
text.DRB: and perceiving also Laban's countenance, that it was not towards him as yesterday and the other day,

text.NRSV: Then the Lord said to Jacob, “Return to the land of your ancestors and to your kindred, and I will be with you.”
text.Rotherham: Then said Yahweh unto Jacob, Return unto the land of thy fathers and to thy kindred,—that I may be with thee.
text.KJV: And the LORD said vnto Iacob, Returne vnto the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred; and I wil be with thee.
text.NABRE: Then the Lord said to Jacob: Return to the land of your ancestors, where you were born, and I will be with you.
text.DRB: especially our Lord saying to him: Return into the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred, and I will be with thee;

text.NRSV: So Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah into the field where his flock was,
text.Rotherham: So Jacob sent, and called for Rachel and for Leah, to the field unto his flock;
text.KJV: And Iacob sent and called Rachel and Leah, to the field vnto his flocke,
text.NABRE: So Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the field where his flock was.
text.DRB: he sent, and called Rachel and Lia into the field where he fed the flocks,

text.NRSV: and said to them, “I see that your father does not regard me as favorably as he did before. But the God of my father has been with me.
text.Rotherham: and said to them, Beholding, am I the face of your father, that it is not towards me as aforetime,—nevertheless, the God of my father, hath been with me;
text.KJV: And said vnto them, I see your fathers countenance, that it is not toward mee as before: but the God of my father hath bene with me.
text.NABRE: There he said to them: “I have noticed that your father’s attitude toward me is not as it was in the past; but the God of my father has been with me.
text.DRB: and said to them: I see your father's countenance that it is not towards me as yesterday and the other day: and the God of my father hath been with me.

text.NRSV: You know that I have served your father with all my strength;
text.Rotherham: and, ye, know that with all my vigour, have I served your father.
text.KJV: And yee know, that with all my power I haue serued your father.
text.NABRE: You know well that with all my strength I served your father;
text.DRB: And yourselves know that I have served your father to the uttermost of my power.

text.NRSV: yet your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times, but God did not permit him to harm me.
text.Rotherham: But, your father, hath deceived me, and hath changed my wages ten times,—yet God hath not suffered him to deal harmfully with me.
text.KJV: And your father hath deceiued mee, and changed my wages ten times: but God suffered him not to hurt me.
text.NABRE: yet your father cheated me and changed my wages ten times. God, however, did not let him do me any harm.
text.DRB: Yea your father also hath circumvented me, and hath changed my wages ten times: and yet God hath not suffered him to hurt me.

text.NRSV: If he said, ‘The speckled shall be your wages,’ then all the flock bore speckled; and if he said, ‘The striped shall be your wages,’ then all the flock bore striped.
text.Rotherham: If thus he saith, The speckled, shall be thy wages, then do all the flock bring forth speckled,—But if thus he saith, The ring-straked, shall be thy wages, then do all the flock bring forth ring-straked.
text.KJV: If hee said thus, The speckled shall be thy wages, then all the cattell bare speckled: and if he said thus, The ring-straked shalbe thy hire, then bare all the cattell ring-straked.
text.NABRE: Whenever your father said, ‘The speckled animals will be your wages,’ the entire flock would bear speckled young; whenever he said, ‘The streaked animals will be your wages,’ the entire flock would bear streaked young.
text.DRB: If at any time he said: They of diverse colours shall be thy wages; all the sheep brought forth young of diverse colours. But when he said contrary: Thou shalt take all the white ones for thy wages; all the flocks brought forth white ones.

text.NRSV: Thus God has taken away the livestock of your father, and given them to me.
text.Rotherham: So hath God stripped away the herds of your father, and given them to me.
text.KJV: Thus God hath taken away the cattell of your father, and giuen them to mee.
text.NABRE: So God took away your father’s livestock and gave it to me.
text.DRB: And God hath taken your father's substance, and given it to me.

text.NRSV: “During the mating of the flock I once had a dream in which I looked up and saw that the male goats that leaped upon the flock were striped, speckled, and mottled.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the season when the flock were in heat, then lifted I mine eyes and beheld in a dream,—and lo! the he-goats that were leaping upon the flock, were ring-straked speckled and dappled.
text.KJV: And it came to passe at the time that the cattell conceiued, that I lifted vp mine eyes and saw in a dreame, and behold, the rammes which leaped vpon the cattell were ring-straked, speckled and grisled.
text.NABRE: Once, during the flock’s mating season, I had a dream in which I saw he-goats mating that were streaked, speckled and mottled.
text.DRB: For after the time came of the ewes conceiving, I lifted up mine eyes, and saw in my sleep the males ascending upon the females of diverse colours, and the spotted, and the speckled.

text.NRSV: Then the angel of God said to me in the dream, ‘Jacob,’ and I said, ‘Here I am!’
text.Rotherham: And the messenger of God said unto me in a dream, Jacob! and I said, Behold me!
text.KJV: And the Angel of God spake vnto me in a dreame, saying, Iacob; And I said, Here am I.
text.NABRE: In the dream God’s angel said to me, ‘Jacob!’ and I replied, ‘Here I am!’
text.DRB: And the Angel of God said to me in sleep: Jacob? And I answered: Here I am.

text.NRSV: And he said, ‘Look up and see that all the goats that leap on the flock are striped, speckled, and mottled; for I have seen all that Laban is doing to you.
text.Rotherham: Then he said Lift, I pray thee, thine eyes and behold All, the he-goats that are leaping upon the flock are ring-straked, speckled, and dappled,—for I have seen all that Laban, has been doing to thee.
text.KJV: And hee said, Lift vp now thine eyes, and see, all the rammes which leape vpon the cattell are ring-straked, speckled and grisled: for I haue seene all that Laban doeth vnto thee.
text.NABRE: Then he said: ‘Look up and see. All the he-goats that are mating are streaked, speckled and mottled, for I have seen all the things that Laban has been doing to you.
text.DRB: Who said: Lift up thine eyes, and see all the males ascending upon the females, them of diverse colours, the spotted and the speckled. For I have seen all things that Laban hath done to thee.

text.NRSV: I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and made a vow to me. Now leave this land at once and return to the land of your birth.’ ”
text.Rotherham: I, am the GOD of Beth-el, where thou didst anoint a pillar, where thou didst vow to me a vow,—Now, rise, go forth out of this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred.
text.KJV: I am the God of Bethel, where thou annoyntedst the pillar, and where thou vowedst a vow vnto mee: now arise, get thee out from this land, and returne vnto the land of thy kindred.
text.NABRE: I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a sacred pillar and made a vow to me. Get up now! Leave this land and return to the land of your birth.’”
text.DRB: I am the God of Bethel, where thou didst anoint the stone, and didst vow the vow unto me. Now therefore arise, and go out of this land, returning into the land of thy nativity.

text.NRSV: Then Rachel and Leah answered him, “Is there any portion or inheritance left to us in our father’s house?
text.Rotherham: Then responded Rachel and Leah, and said to him,—Have we any longer a portion or an inheritance in the house of our father?
text.KJV: And Rachel and Leah answered, and said vnto him; Is there yet any portion or inheritance for vs in our fathers house?
text.NABRE: Rachel and Leah answered him: “Do we still have an heir’s portion in our father’s house?
text.DRB: And Rachel and Lia answered: Have we any thing left in the goods and heritage of our father's house?

text.NRSV: Are we not regarded by him as foreigners? For he has sold us, and he has been using up the money given for us.
text.Rotherham: Are we not accounted, aliens, to him seeing that having sold us, he hath then gone on devouring, our silver?
text.KJV: Are we not counted of him strangers? for he hath sold vs, and hath quite deuoured also our money.
text.NABRE: Are we not regarded by him as outsiders? He not only sold us; he has even used up the money that he got for us!
text.DRB: Hath he not reputed us as strangers, and sold us, and eaten up the price of us?

text.NRSV: All the property that God has taken away from our father belongs to us and to our children; now then, do whatever God has said to you.”
text.Rotherham: Surely all the riches which God hath stripped off from our father, unto us, it belongeth, and unto our sons,—Now, therefore, whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do.
text.KJV: For all the riches which God hath taken from our father, that is ours, and our childrens: now then whatsoeuer God hath said vnto thee, doe.
text.NABRE: All the wealth that God took away from our father really belongs to us and our children. So do whatever God has told you.”
text.DRB: But God hath taken our father's riches, and delivered them to us, and to our children: wherefore do all things that God hath commanded thee.

text.NRSV: So Jacob arose, and set his children and his wives on camels;
text.Rotherham: So Jacob arose,—and mounted his sons and his wives on the camels;
text.KJV: ¶ Then Iacob rose vp, and set his sonnes and his wiues vpon camels.
text.NABRE: Jacob proceeded to put his children and wives on camels,
text.DRB: Jacob therefore rose up, and setting his children and wives upon camels, went his way.

text.NRSV: and he drove away all his livestock, all the property that he had gained, the livestock in his possession that he had acquired in Paddan-aram, to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: and drave away all his herds and all his goods which he had gathered, the gains he had gained, which he had gathered in Padan-aram; that he might go in unto Isaac his father unto the land of Canaan.
text.KJV: And he caried away all his cattell, and all his goods which he had gotten, the cattell of his getting, which hee had gotten in Padan Aram, for to goe to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan.
text.NABRE: and he drove off all his livestock and all the property he had acquired in Paddan-aram, to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
text.DRB: And he took all his substance, and flocks, and whatsoever he had gotten in Mesopotamia, and went forward to Isaac his father into the land of Chanaan.

text.NRSV: Now Laban had gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole her father’s household gods.
text.Rotherham: Now, Laban, had gone, to shear his sheep,—so Rachel stole the household gods that belonged to her father,
text.KJV: And Laban went to sheare his sheepe: and Rachel had stollen the Images that were her fathers.
text.NABRE: Now Laban was away shearing his sheep, and Rachel had stolen her father’s household images.
text.DRB: At that time Laban was gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole the idols of her father.

text.NRSV: And Jacob deceived Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he intended to flee.
text.Rotherham: Thus Jacob stole away unawares to Laban, the Syrian,—in that he had not told him that he was about to flee.
text.KJV: And Iacob stale away vnawares to Laban the Syrian, in that he told him not that he fled.
text.NABRE: Jacob had hoodwinked Laban the Aramean by not telling him that he was going to flee.
text.DRB: And Jacob would not confess to his father in law that he fled.

text.NRSV: So he fled with all that he had; starting out he crossed the Euphrates, and set his face toward the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So he fled, he—and all that he had, and he arose, and passed over the River,—and set his face towards the mountain of Gilead.
text.KJV: So hee fled with all that hee had, and he rose vp and passed ouer the Riuer, and set his face toward the mount Gilead.
text.NABRE: Thus he fled with all that he had. Once he was across the Euphrates, he headed for the hill country of Gilead.
text.DRB: And when he was gone as well himself as all things that were his right, & having passed the river was marching on to the Mount Galaad,

text.NRSV: On the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
text.Rotherham: And it was told Laban, on the third day,—that Jacob had fled.
text.KJV: And it was tolde Laban on the third day, that Iacob was fled.
text.NABRE: On the third day, word came to Laban that Jacob had fled.
text.DRB: it was told Laban the third day that Jacob fled.

text.NRSV: So he took his kinsfolk with him and pursued him for seven days until he caught up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him a journey of seven days—and overtook him in the mountain of Gilead.
text.KJV: And hee tooke his brethren with him, and pursued after him seuen dayes iourney, and they ouertooke him in the mount Gilead.
text.NABRE: Taking his kinsmen with him, he pursued him for seven days until he caught up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text.DRB: Who, taking his brethren unto him, pursued him seven days: and he overtook him in the Mount Galaad.

text.NRSV: But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream by night, and said to him, “Take heed that you say not a word to Jacob, either good or bad.”
text.Rotherham: And God went in unto Laban the Syrian in a dream of the night,—and said to him Take heed to thyself that thou speak not with Jacob either good or bad.
text.KJV: And God came to Laban the Syrian in a dreame by night, and saide vnto him, Take heed that thou speake not to Iacob either good or bad.
text.NABRE: But that night God appeared to Laban the Aramean in a dream and said to him: Take care not to say anything to Jacob.
text.DRB: And he saw in his sleep God saying unto him: Take heed thou speak not roughly any thing against Jacob.

text.NRSV: Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the hill country, and Laban with his kinsfolk camped in the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So then Laban came up with Jacob,—Jacob, having pitched his tent in the mountain, Laban, also, with his brethren pitched in the mountain of Gilead.
text.KJV: ¶ Then Laban ouertooke Iacob. Now Iacob had pitched his tent in the mount: and Laban with his brethren pitched in the mount of Gilead.
text.NABRE: When Laban overtook Jacob, Jacob’s tents were pitched in the hill country; Laban also pitched his tents in the hill country of Gilead.
text.DRB: And Jacob had now pitched his tent in the mountain: and when he with his brethren had overtaken him, he pitched his tent in the same Mount Galaad.

text.NRSV: Laban said to Jacob, “What have you done? You have deceived me, and carried away my daughters like captives of the sword.
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban to Jacob, What hadst thou done, that thou shouldst steal away unawares to me,—and shouldst carry off my daughters, as captives of the sword?
text.KJV: And Laban said to Iacob, What hast thou done, that thou hast stollen away vnawares to me, and caried away my daughters, as captiues taken with the sword?
text.NABRE: Laban said to Jacob, “How could you hoodwink me and carry off my daughters like prisoners of war?
text.DRB: And he said to Jacob: Why didst thou so, that unwitting to me thou wouldst carry away my daughters as captives with the sword?

text.NRSV: Why did you flee secretly and deceive me and not tell me? I would have sent you away with mirth and songs, with tambourine and lyre.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore didst thou secretly flee, and rob me,—and didst not tell me, that I might send thee away with rejoicing and with songs, with timbrel and with lyre;
text.KJV: Wherefore didst thou flie away secretly, and steale away from me, and didst not tell mee? that I might haue sent thee away with mirth, and with songs, with tabret, and with harpe,
text.NABRE: Why did you dupe me by stealing away secretly? You did not tell me! I would have sent you off with joyful singing to the sound of tambourines and harps.
text.DRB: Why wouldst thou fly without my knowledge, and not tell me, that I might have brought thee on the way with joy, and songs, and timbrels, and citterns?

text.NRSV: And why did you not permit me to kiss my sons and my daughters farewell? What you have done is foolish.
text.Rotherham: and didst not suffer me to kiss my sons and my daughters? Now, hast thou done, foolishly.
text.KJV: And hast not suffered me to kisse my sonnes and my daughters? thou hast now done foolishly in so doing.
text.NABRE: You did not even allow me a parting kiss to my daughters and grandchildren! Now what you have done makes no sense.
text.DRB: Thou hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and daughters: thou hast done foolishly: now also indeed,

text.NRSV: It is in my power to do you harm; but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying, ‘Take heed that you speak to Jacob neither good nor bad.’
text.Rotherham: It is in the power of my hand, to deal with thee for harm,—but the God of your father, last night, spake unto me, saying, Take heed to thyself that thou speak not with Jacob, either good or bad.
text.KJV: It is in the power of my hand to doe you hurt: but the God of your father spake vnto mee yesternight, saying, Take thou heed, that thou speake not to Iacob either good or bad.
text.NABRE: I have it in my power to harm all of you; but last night the God of your father said to me, ‘Take care not to say anything to Jacob!’
text.DRB: my hand is able to requite thee evil: but the God of your father said unto me yesterday: Take heed thou speak not any thing against Jacob roughly.

text.NRSV: Even though you had to go because you longed greatly for your father’s house, why did you steal my gods?”
text.Rotherham: But, now, though thou, didst even take thy journey, because thou, didst sorely long, for the house of thy father, wherefore didst thou steal away my gods?
text.KJV: And now though thou wouldest needes bee gone, because thou sore longedst after thy fathers house; yet wherefore hast thou stollen my gods?
text.NABRE: Granted that you had to leave because you were longing for your father’s house, why did you steal my gods?”
text.DRB: Suppose thou didst desire to go to thy friends, and hadst a longing to thy father's house, why didst thou steal my gods?

text.NRSV: Jacob answered Laban, “Because I was afraid, for I thought that you would take your daughters from me by force.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob answered and said unto Laban,—Because I was afraid, for I said, Lest thou take by force thy daughters from me!
text.KJV: And Iacob answered and said to Laban, Because I was afraid: for I said, Peraduenture thou wouldest take by force thy daughters from me.
text.NABRE: Jacob replied to Laban, “I was frightened at the thought that you might take your daughters away from me by force.
text.DRB: Jacob answered: In that I departed unwitting to thee, I feared lest thou wouldst take away thy daughters by force.

text.NRSV: But anyone with whom you find your gods shall not live. In the presence of our kinsfolk, point out what I have that is yours, and take it.” Now Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen the gods.
text.Rotherham: He with whom thou find thy gods, shall not live! Before our brethren, note thou for thyself what is with me and take what is thine. Neither did Jacob know that, Rachel, had stolen them.
text.KJV: With whomsoeuer thou findest thy gods, let him not liue: before our brethren discerne thou what is thine with me, and take it to thee: for Iacob knew not that Rachel had stollen them.
text.NABRE: As for your gods, the one you find them with shall not remain alive! If, with our kinsmen looking on, you identify anything here as belonging to you, take it.” Jacob had no idea that Rachel had stolen the household images.
text.DRB: But whereas thou chargest me with theft; with whom soever thou shalt find thy gods, let him be slain before our brethren: search whatsoever of thy things thou shalt find with me, and take away. Saying this, he knew not that Rachel had stolen the idols.

text.NRSV: So Laban went into Jacob’s tent, and into Leah’s tent, and into the tent of the two maids, but he did not find them. And he went out of Leah’s tent, and entered Rachel’s.
text.Rotherham: Then entered Laban into the tent of Jacob and into the tent of Leah and into the tent of the two handmaids, and found nothing,—then went he forth out of the tent of Leah, and entered into the tent of Rachel.
text.KJV: And Laban went into Iacobs tent, and into Leahs tent, and into the two maid seruants tents: but he found them not. Then went he out of Leahs tent, and entred into Rachels tent.
text.NABRE: Laban then went in and searched Jacob’s tent and Leah’s tent, as well as the tents of the two maidservants; but he did not find them. Leaving Leah’s tent, he went into Rachel’s.
text.DRB: Laban therefore having gone into the tent of Jacob, and of Lia, and of both the handmaids, found them not. And when he was entered into Rachel's tent,

text.NRSV: Now Rachel had taken the household gods and put them in the camel’s saddle, and sat on them. Laban felt all about in the tent, but did not find them.
text.Rotherham: Now Rachel, had taken the household gods and put them in the basket-saddle of the camel, and taken her seat upon them. And Laban felt about throughout all the tent, and found them not.
text.KJV: Now Rachel had taken the images, and put them in the camels furniture, and sate vpon them: and Laban searched all the tent, but found them not.
text.NABRE: Meanwhile Rachel had taken the household images, put them inside the camel’s saddlebag, and seated herself upon them. When Laban had rummaged through her whole tent without finding them,
text.DRB: she in haste hid the idols under the camel's litter, and sat thereupon: and when he had sought all the tent, and found nothing,

text.NRSV: And she said to her father, “Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise before you, for the way of women is upon me.” So he searched, but did not find the household gods.
text.Rotherham: And she said unto her father, Let it not be vexing in the eyes of my lord that I cannot rise up at thy presence, for, the way of women, is upon me. So he made search, but found not the household gods,
text.KJV: And shee said to her father, Let it not displease my lord, that I cannot rise vp before thee; for the custome of women is vpon mee: and he searched, but found not the images.
text.NABRE: she said to her father, “Do not let my lord be angry that I cannot rise in your presence; I am having my period.” So, despite his search, he did not find the household images.
text.DRB: she said: Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise up before thee, because according to the custom of women it is now chanced to me. So his carefulness in seeking was deluded.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob became angry, and upbraided Laban. Jacob said to Laban, “What is my offense? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?
text.Rotherham: And it was vexing to Jacob and he contended with Laban,—and Jacob responded and said to Laban, What was my trespass, what my sin, that thou shouldst have come burning after me?
text.KJV: ¶ And Iacob was wroth, and chode with Laban: and Iacob answered and said to Laban, what is my trespasse? what is my sinne, that thou hast so hotly pursued after me?
text.NABRE: Jacob, now angered, confronted Laban and demanded, “What crime or offense have I committed that you should hound me?
text.DRB: And Jacob being angry said in chiding manner: For what fault of mine, and for what offence of my part hast thou so chased after me,

text.NRSV: Although you have felt about through all my goods, what have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsfolk and your kinsfolk, so that they may decide between us two.
text.Rotherham: Now that thou hast felt about among all my goods, what hast thou found of the goods of thine own house? Set it out here, right before my brethren and thy brethren,—that they may give sentence betwixt us two.
text.KJV: Whereas thou hast searched all my stuffe, what hast thou found of all thy houshold stuffe? set it here before my brethren, and thy brethren, that they may iudge betwixt vs both.
text.NABRE: Now that you have rummaged through all my things, what have you found from your household belongings? Produce it here before your kinsmen and mine, and let them decide between the two of us.
text.DRB: and searched all my household-stuff? What hast thou found of all the substance of thy house? Lay it here before my brethren, and thy brethren, and let them judge between me and thee.

text.NRSV: These twenty years I have been with you; your ewes and your female goats have not miscarried, and I have not eaten the rams of your flocks.
text.Rotherham: This twenty years’ time, have, I, been with thee—Thy ewes and thy she-goats, have not cast their young,—And the rams of thy flock, have I not eaten,
text.KJV: This twentie yeeres haue I bene with thee: thy ewes and thy shee goates haue not cast their yong, and the rammes of thy flocke haue I not eaten.
text.NABRE: “In the twenty years that I was under you, no ewe or she-goat of yours ever miscarried, and I have never eaten rams of your flock.
text.DRB: Have I therefore been with thee twenty years? Thy ewes and goats were not barren, the wethers of thy flock I did not eat:

text.NRSV: That which was torn by wild beasts I did not bring to you; I bore the loss of it myself; of my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.
text.Rotherham: The torn, have I not brought in unto thee—I bare the loss, At my hand, didst thou require it,—Stolen by day, or stolen by night.
text.KJV: That which was torne of beasts, I brought not vnto thee: I bare the losse of it; of my hand didst thou require it, whether stollen by day, or stollen by night.
text.NABRE: I never brought you an animal torn by wild beasts; I made good the loss myself. You held me responsible for anything stolen by day or night.
text.DRB: neither that which the beast had caught did I shew to thee, I made good all the damage: whatsoever perished by theft thou didst exact it of me:

text.NRSV: It was like this with me: by day the heat consumed me, and the cold by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes.
text.Rotherham: Thus have I been—By day, drought hath consumed me, And, frost, by, night,—So that my sleep fled away from mine eyes.
text.KJV: Thus I was in þ day, the drought consumed mee, and the frost by night, aud my sleep departed from mine eyes.
text.NABRE: Often the scorching heat devoured me by day, and the frost by night, while sleep fled from my eyes!
text.DRB: day and night was I parched with heat, and with frost, & sleep did fly from mine eyes.

text.NRSV: These twenty years I have been in your house; I served you fourteen years for your two daughters, and six years for your flock, and you have changed my wages ten times.
text.Rotherham: This, hath been my lot twenty years in thy house,—I served thee fourteen years for thy two daughters, And six years for thy sheep—And thou didst change my wages ten times:—
text.KJV: Thus have I bene twentie yeres in thy house: I serued thee fourteene yeeres for thy two daughters, and sixe yeres for thy cattel; and thou hast changed my wages ten times.
text.NABRE: Of the twenty years that I have now spent in your household, I served you fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your flock, while you changed my wages ten times.
text.DRB: And in this sort have I served thee in thy house twenty years, fourteen for thy daughters, and six for thy flocks: thou hast changed also my wages ten times.

text.NRSV: If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been on my side, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. God saw my affliction and the labor of my hands, and rebuked you last night.”
text.Rotherham: But that the God of my father—The God of Abraham, and the Dread of Isaac—Proved to be mine, Surely even now, empty, hadst thou let me go? My humiliation and the wearying toil of my hands, God had seen, And therefore gave sentence last night!
text.KJV: Except the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the feare of Isaac had bin with me, surely thou hadst sent me away now emptie: God hath seene mine affliction, and the labour of my hands, & rebuked thee yesternight.
text.NABRE: If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been on my side, you would now have sent me away empty-handed. But God saw my plight and the fruits of my toil, and last night he reproached you.”
text.DRB: Unless the God of my father Abraham, and the fear of Isaac had holpen me, peradventure now thou hadst sent me away naked: God beheld my affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked thee yesterday.

text.NRSV: Then Laban answered and said to Jacob, “The daughters are my daughters, the children are my children, the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine. But what can I do today about these daughters of mine, or about their children whom they have borne?
text.Rotherham: Then responded Laban and said unto Jacob—the daughters, are my daughters, And the sons, are my sons, And the sheep are my sheep, And, all that, thou, beholdest, To me doth it belong! But to my daughters, what can I do to these this day, Or to their sons whom they have borne?
text.KJV: ¶ And Laban answered and said vnto Iacob, These daughters are my daughters, and these children are my children, and these cattell are my cattell, and all that thou seest, is mine: and what can I doe this day vnto these my daughters, or vnto their children which they haue borne?
text.NABRE: Laban replied to Jacob: “The daughters are mine, their children are mine, and the flocks are mine; everything you see belongs to me. What can I do now for my own daughters and for the children they have borne?
text.DRB: Laban answered him: The daughters are mine and the children, and thy flocks, and all things that thou seest are mine: what can I do to my daughters, and nephews?

text.NRSV: Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I; and let it be a witness between you and me.”
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, come on! Let us solemnise a covenant, I and thou,—So shall it be a witness betwixt me and thee.
text.KJV: Now therefore come thou, let vs make a couenant, I and thou: and let it be for a witnesse betweene me and thee.
text.NABRE: Come, now, let us make a covenant, you and I; and it will be a treaty between you and me.”
text.DRB: Come therefore, let us enter in league: that it may be for a testimony between me and thee.

text.NRSV: So Jacob took a stone, and set it up as a pillar.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob took a stone,—and set it up as a pillar.
text.KJV: And Iacob tooke a stone, and set it vp for a pillar.
text.NABRE: Then Jacob took a stone and set it up as a sacred pillar.
text.DRB: Jacob therefore took a stone, and erected it for a title:

text.NRSV: And Jacob said to his kinsfolk, “Gather stones,” and they took stones, and made a heap; and they ate there by the heap.
text.Rotherham: Then said Jacob to his brethren—Gather stones. And they took stones and made a heap,—and they did eat there, by the heap.
text.KJV: And Iacob saide vnto his brethren, Gather stones: and they tooke stones, and made an heape, and they did eate there vpon the heape.
text.NABRE: Jacob said to his kinsmen, “Gather stones.” So they got stones and made a mound; and they ate there at the mound.
text.DRB: and he said to his brethren: Bring hither stones. Who gathering them together made a heap, and they did eat upon it:

text.NRSV: Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha: but Jacob called it Galeed.
text.Rotherham: And Laban called it, Jegar-sahadutha,—but, Jacob, called it, Galeed.
text.KJV: And Laban called it Iegar-Sahadutha: but Iacob called it Galeed.
text.NABRE: Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob called it Galeed.
text.DRB: Which Laban called the witness-heap: and Jacob called the hillock of testimony, either of men according to the property of his language.

text.NRSV: Laban said, “This heap is a witness between you and me today.” Therefore he called it Galeed,
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban: This heap, be witness betwixt me, and thee, to-day. For this cause, is the name thereof called Galeed;
text.KJV: And Laban said, This heape is a witnesse betweene mee and thee this day. Therefore was the name of it called Galeed,
text.NABRE: Laban said, “This mound will be a witness from now on between you and me.” That is why it was named Galeed—
text.DRB: And Laban said: This heap shall be a witness between me and thee this day, and therefore the name thereof was called Galaad, that is, The witness-heap.

text.NRSV: and the pillar Mizpah, for he said, “The Lord watch between you and me, when we are absent one from the other.
text.Rotherham: also, The Watch Tower, as to which it is said, Yahweh watch betwixt me and thee, when we are parted one from another.
text.KJV: And Mizpah: for he said, The LORD watch betweene me and thee when we are absent one from another.
text.NABRE: and also Mizpah, for he said: “May the Lord keep watch between you and me when we are out of each other’s sight.
text.DRB: Our Lord behold and judge us when we shall be departed one from the other,

text.NRSV: If you ill-treat my daughters, or if you take wives in addition to my daughters, though no one else is with us, remember that God is witness between you and me.”
text.Rotherham: If thou oppress my daughters, or if thou take wives besides my daughters,—there may be no man with us—see! God, be witness betwixt me, and thee!
text.KJV: If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wiues beside my daughters, no man is with vs; See, God is witnesse betwixt mee and thee.
text.NABRE: If you mistreat my daughters, or take other wives besides my daughters, know that even though no one else is there, God will be a witness between you and me.”
text.DRB: if thou shalt afflict my daughters, and if thou bring in other wives over them; none is witness of our talk but God, who is present and beholdeth.

text.NRSV: Then Laban said to Jacob, “See this heap and see the pillar, which I have set between you and me.
text.Rotherham: And Laban said to Jacob—Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have set betwixt me, and thee:
text.KJV: And Laban said to Iacob, Behold this heape, and behold this pillar, which I haue cast betwixt me and thee.
text.NABRE: Laban said further to Jacob: “Here is this mound, and here is the sacred pillar that I have set up between you and me.
text.DRB: And he said again to Jacob: Behold this heap, and the stone which I have erected between me and thee,

text.NRSV: This heap is a witness, and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass beyond this heap to you, and you will not pass beyond this heap and this pillar to me, for harm.
text.Rotherham: A witness, be this heap, and, a witness, the pillar,—That, I, am not to pass, unto thee, over this heap, And, thou, art not to pass, unto me, over this heap and this pillar for harm.
text.KJV: This heape be witnesse, and this pillar be witnesse, that I will not passe ouer this heape to thee, and that thou shalt not passe ouer this heape, and this pillar vnto me, for harme.
text.NABRE: This mound will be a witness, and this sacred pillar will be a witness, that, with hostile intent, I may not pass beyond this mound into your territory, nor may you pass beyond it into mine.
text.DRB: shall be a witness: this heap, I say, and the stone, be they for a testimony, if either I shall pass beyond it going towards thee, or thou shalt pass beyond it, thinking harm to me.

text.NRSV: May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor”—the God of their father—“judge between us.” So Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac,
text.Rotherham: The Gods of Abraham and the Gods of Nahor judge betwixt us—the Gods of their fathers. And Jacob sware by the Dread of his father Isaac:
text.KJV: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, iudge betwixt vs. And Iacob sware by the feare of his father Isaac.
text.NABRE: May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us!” Jacob took the oath by the Fear of his father Isaac.
text.DRB: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nachor judge between us, the God of their father, Jacob therefore sware by the fear of his father Isaac:

text.NRSV: and Jacob offered a sacrifice on the height and called his kinsfolk to eat bread; and they ate bread and tarried all night in the hill country.
text.Rotherham: and Jacob offered sacrifice in the mountain, and called on his brethren to eat bread. So they did eat bread, and tarried the night in the mountain.
text.KJV: Then Iacob offred sacrifice vpon the mount, and called his brethren to eate bread, and they did eate bread, and taried all night in the mount.
text.NABRE: He then offered a sacrifice on the mountain and invited his kinsmen to share in the meal. When they had eaten, they passed the night on the mountain.
text.DRB: and after he had offered victims in the mountain, he called his brethren to eat bread. Who when they had eaten, lodged there.

text.NRSV: Early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them; then he departed and returned home.
text.Rotherham: And Laban rose up early in the morning, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban went his way and returned to his place.
text.KJV: And earely in the morning, Laban rose vp and kissed his sonnes, and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban departed, and returned vnto his place.
text.DRB: but Laban arising in the night, kissed his sons, and daughters, and blessed them: and returned unto his place.

text.NRSV: Jacob went on his way and the angels of God met him;
text.Rotherham: When, Jacob, had gone on his way, there met him, messengers of God.
text.KJV: And Iacob went on his way, and the Angels of God met him.
text.NABRE: Early the next morning, Laban kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them; then he set out on his journey back home.
text.DRB: Jacob also went on his journey that he had begun: and the Angels of God met him.

text.NRSV: and when Jacob saw them he said, “This is God’s camp!” So he called that place Mahanaim.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said, when he saw them, the camp of God, is this! So he called the name of that place, Mahanaim.
text.KJV: And when Iacob saw them, he said, This is Gods hoste: and hee called the name of that place Mahanaim.
text.NABRE: Meanwhile Jacob continued on his own way, and God’s angels encountered him.
text.DRB: Whom when he had seen, he said: These are the Camps of God, and he called the name of that place Mahanaim, that is, Camps.

text.NRSV: Jacob sent messengers before him to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the country of Edom,
text.Rotherham: Then did Jacob send messengers before him unto Esau his brother, to the land of Seir, the field of Edom.
text.KJV: And Iacob sent messengers before him, to Esau his brother, vnto the land of Seir, the countrey of Edom.
text.NABRE: When Jacob saw them he said, “This is God’s encampment.” So he named that place Mahanaim.
text.DRB: And he sent also messengers before him to Esau his brother into the land of Seir, into the country of Edom:

text.NRSV: instructing them, “Thus you shall say to my lord Esau: Thus says your servant Jacob, ‘I have lived with Laban as an alien, and stayed until now;
text.Rotherham: And he commanded them, saying, Thus, shall ye say to my lord to Esau, Thus, saith thy servant Jacob, With Laban, have I sojourned, and tarried until now:
text.KJV: And he commaunded them, saying, Thus shall ye speake vnto my lord Esau, Thy seruant Iacob saith thus, I haue soiourned with Laban, and stayed there vntill now.
text.NABRE: Jacob sent messengers ahead to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the country of Edom,
text.DRB: and he commanded them, saying: Thus speak ye unto my lord Esau: This saith thy brother Jacob: I have sojourned, and have been with Laban until this present day.

text.NRSV: and I have oxen, donkeys, flocks, male and female slaves; and I have sent to tell my lord, in order that I may find favor in your sight.’ ”
text.Rotherham: And I have oxen and asses, flocks and men-servants, and maid-servants, So I must needs send to tell my lord, that I might find favour in thine eyes.
text.KJV: And I haue oxen, and asses, flockes, and men seruants and women seruants: and I haue sent to tell my lord, that I may find grace in thy sight.
text.NABRE: ordering them: “Thus you shall say to my lord Esau: ‘Thus says your servant Jacob: I have been residing with Laban and have been delayed until now.
text.DRB: I have oxen, & asses, & sheep, & men servants: & women servants: & now I send a legacy to my lord, that I may find grace in thy sight.

text.NRSV: The messengers returned to Jacob, saying, “We came to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him.”
text.Rotherham: And the messengers returned unto Jacob, saying,—We came in unto thy brother unto Esau, moreover also he is on his way to meet thee, and four hundred men with him.
text.KJV: ¶ And the messengers returned to Iacob, saying, Wee came to thy brother Esau, and also he commeth to meet thee, and foure hundred men with him.
text.NABRE: I own oxen, donkeys and sheep, as well as male and female servants. I have sent my lord this message in the hope of gaining your favor.’”
text.DRB: And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying: We came to Esau thy brother, & behold he cometh with speed to meet thee with four hundred men.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed; and he divided the people that were with him, and the flocks and herds and camels, into two companies,
text.Rotherham: Then was Jacob greatly afraid, and in distress. So he divided the people that were with him and the flocks and the herds and the camels into two camps,
text.KJV: Then Iacob was greatly afraid, and distressed, and he diuided the people that was with him, and the flockes, and herdes, and the camels into two bands,
text.NABRE: When the messengers returned to Jacob, they said, “We found your brother Esau. He is now coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him.”
text.DRB: Jacob feared exceedingly: and being sore afraid divided the people that was with him, the flocks also and the sheep, and the oxen, and the camels, into two troops,

text.NRSV: thinking, “If Esau comes to the one company and destroys it, then the company that is left will escape.”
text.Rotherham: And he said. Should Esau come upon the one camp, and smite it, yet shall the camp that is left escape.
text.KJV: And said, If Esau come to the one company, and smite it, then the other company which is left, shall escape.
