text.DRB: And our Lord was with Joseph, and having mercy upon him gave him grace in the sight of the chief of the prison.

text.NRSV: The chief jailer committed to Joseph’s care all the prisoners who were in the prison, and whatever was done there, he was the one who did it.
text.Rotherham: So the chief of the prison delivered up, into Joseph’s hand all the prisoners who were in the prison,—and of all that was doing there, he, was the doer.
text.KJV: And the keeper of the prison committed to Iosephs hand all the prisoners that were in the prison, and whatsoeuer they did there, he was the doer of it:
text.NABRE: The chief jailer put Joseph in charge of all the prisoners in the jail. Everything that had to be done there, he was the one to do it.
text.DRB: Who delivered in his hand all the prisoners that were kept in custody: and whatsoever was done, was under him.

text.NRSV: The chief jailer paid no heed to anything that was in Joseph’s care, because the Lord was with him; and whatever he did, the Lord made it prosper.
text.Rotherham: After nothing, was the chief of the prison looking, of whatever was in his hand,—in that Yahweh, was with him, and whatsoever, he was doing, Yahweh, was causing to prosper.
text.KJV: The keeper of the prison looked not to any thing, that was vnder his hand, because the LORD was with him: & that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper.
text.NABRE: The chief jailer did not have to look after anything that was in Joseph’s charge, since the Lord was with him and was bringing success to whatever he was doing.
text.DRB: Neither did himself know anything, having committed all things to him: for our Lord was with him, and directed all his works.

text.NRSV: Some time after this, the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and his baker offended their lord the king of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt, and the baker, had sinned against their lord the king of Egypt.
text.KJV: And it came to passe after these things, that the Butler of the King of Egypt, and his Baker, had offended their lord the King of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Some time afterward, the royal cupbearer and baker offended their lord, the king of Egypt.
text.DRB: These things being so done, it chanced that two Eunuchs, the cup-bearer of the king of Ægypt, and his baker, offended against their lord.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker,
text.Rotherham: Then fell the wroth of Pharaoh upon his two courtiers, upon the chief of the butlers, and upon the chief of the bakers;
text.KJV: And Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chiefe of the Butlers, and against the chiefe of the Bakers.
text.NABRE: Pharaoh was angry with his two officials, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker,
text.DRB: And Pharao being wrath against them (for the one was chief of the cup-bearers, the other chief baker)

text.NRSV: and he put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the prison where Joseph was confined.
text.Rotherham: so he delivered them up into the ward of the house of the chief of the royal executioners, into the prison,—the place where Joseph was imprisoned.
text.KJV: And he put them in ward in the house of the captaine of the guard, into the prison, the place where Ioseph was bound.
text.NABRE: and he put them in custody in the house of the chief steward, the same jail where Joseph was confined.
text.DRB: he sent them into the prison of the captain of the soldiers, in the which Joseph also was prisoner.

text.NRSV: The captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he waited on them; and they continued for some time in custody.
text.Rotherham: And the chief of the royal executioners charged Joseph with them and he waited upon them, and they were some days in ward.
text.KJV: And the captaine of the guard charged Ioseph with them, and he serued them, and they continued a season in warde.
text.NABRE: The chief steward assigned Joseph to them, and he became their attendant. After they had been in custody for some time,
text.DRB: But the keeper of the prison delivered them to Joseph, who also ministered to them: some little time was passed, and they were kept in custody.

text.NRSV: One night they both dreamed—the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, who were confined in the prison—each his own dream, and each dream with its own meaning.
text.Rotherham: And they dreamed a dream they two, each man, his dream in one night, each man, according to the interpretation of his dream,—the butler and the baker who belonged to the king of Egypt, who were imprisoned in the prison.
text.KJV: ¶ And they dreamed a dreame both of them, each man his dreame in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dreame, the Butler and the Baker of the king of Egypt, which were bound in the prison.
text.NABRE: the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt who were confined in the jail both had dreams on the same night, each his own dream and each dream with its own meaning.
text.DRB: And they saw each of them both a dream in one night, according to an interpretation agreeing to themselves:

text.NRSV: When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw that they were troubled.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph came in unto them in the morning,—and looked at them, and lo! they were sad.
text.KJV: And Ioseph came in vnto them in the morning, and looked vpon them, and behold, they were sad.
text.NABRE: When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw that they looked disturbed.
text.DRB: to whom when Joseph was entered in the morning, and saw them sad,

text.NRSV: So he asked Pharaoh’s officers, who were with him in custody in his master’s house, “Why are your faces downcast today?”
text.Rotherham: So he asked Pharaoh’s courtiers who were with him in the ward of his lord’s house, saying, Why are your faces troubled to-day!
text.KJV: And he asked Pharaohs officers that were with him in the warde of his lords house, saying, wherefore looke ye so sadly to day?
text.NABRE: So he asked Pharaoh’s officials who were with him in custody in his master’s house, “Why do you look so troubled today?”
text.DRB: he asked them, saying: Why is your countenance sadder today than it was wont?

text.NRSV: They said to him, “We have had dreams, and there is no one to interpret them.” And Joseph said to them, “Do not interpretations belong to God? Please tell them to me.”
text.Rotherham: And they said unto him, A dream, have we dreamed, but there is none, to interpret, it. Then said Joseph unto them—Is it not unto God, that interpretations belong? Relate it. I pray you, to me.
text.KJV: And they said vnto him, we haue dreamed a dreame, and there is no interpreter of it. And Ioseph said vnto them, Doe not interpretations belong to God? tell me them, I pray you.
text.NABRE: They answered him, “We have had dreams, but there is no one to interpret them.” Joseph said to them, “Do interpretations not come from God? Please tell me the dreams.”
text.DRB: Who answered: We have seen a dream, and there is nobody to interpret it to us. And Joseph said to them: Why doth not interpretation belong to God? Tell me what you have seen.

text.NRSV: So the chief cupbearer told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, “In my dream there was a vine before me,
text.Rotherham: And the chief of the butlers related his dream to Joseph,—and said to him, In my dream, then lo! a vine, before me;
text.KJV: And the chiefe Butler tolde his dreame to Ioseph, and said to him; In my dreame, beholde, a vine was before mee:
text.NABRE: Then the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream. “In my dream,” he said, “I saw a vine in front of me,
text.DRB: The chief of the cup-bearers first told his dream: I saw before me a vine,

text.NRSV: and on the vine there were three branches. As soon as it budded, its blossoms came out and the clusters ripened into grapes.
text.Rotherham: and, in the vine, three shoots,—and the same at sprouting time, had shot up her blossom, and her clusters had brought to perfection ripe grapes.
text.KJV: And in the vine were three branches, and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot foorth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes.
text.NABRE: and on the vine were three branches. It had barely budded when its blossoms came out, and its clusters ripened into grapes.
text.DRB: wherein were three branches, growing by little and little into buds, and after the blossoms, the grapes waxed ripe:

text.NRSV: Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand; and I took the grapes and pressed them into Pharaoh’s cup, and placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand.”
text.Rotherham: Now, the cup of Pharaoh, being in my hand, I took the ripe grapes and pressed them out into the cup of Pharaoh, and set the cup upon Pharaoh’s palm.
text.KJV: And Pharaohs cup was in my hand, and I tooke the grapes and pressed them into Pharaohs cup: and I gaue the cup into Pharaohs hand.
text.NABRE: Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand; so I took the grapes, pressed them out into his cup, and put it in Pharaoh’s hand.”
text.DRB: and the cup of Pharao in my hand: and I took the grapes, and wrung them into the cup which I held, and I gave the cup to Pharao.

text.NRSV: Then Joseph said to him, “This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days;
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said to him, This, is the interpretation thereof,—The three shoots are three days:
text.KJV: And Ioseph said vnto him, This is the interpretation of it: the three branches are three dayes,
text.NABRE: Joseph said to him: “This is its interpretation. The three branches are three days;
text.DRB: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three branches, are yet three days:

text.NRSV: within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your office; and you shall place Pharaoh’s cup in his hand, just as you used to do when you were his cupbearer.
text.Rotherham: In three days more, will Pharaoh lift up thy head, and restore thee to thine office, and thou shalt set the cup of Pharaoh in his hand, according to the former custom when thou wast his butler.
text.KJV: Yet within three dayes shall Pharaoh lift vp thine head, and restore thee vnto thy place, and thou shalt deliuer Pharaohs cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his Butler.
text.NABRE: within three days Pharaoh will single you out and restore you to your post. You will be handing Pharaoh his cup as you formerly did when you were his cupbearer.
text.DRB: after the which Pharao will remember thy service, & will restore thee to thy old degree: & thou shalt give him the cup according to thine office, as before thou hadst wont to do.

text.NRSV: But remember me when it is well with you; please do me the kindness to make mention of me to Pharaoh, and so get me out of this place.
text.Rotherham: But if thou remember how I was with thee when it shall go well with thee, then wilt thou I pray thee do me a lovingkindness,—and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me forth out of this house;
text.KJV: But thinke on me, when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindenesse, I pray thee, vnto mee, and make mention of me vnto Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house.
text.NABRE: Only think of me when all is well with you, and please do me the great favor of mentioning me to Pharaoh, to get me out of this place.
text.DRB: Only remember me, when it shall be well with thee, and do me this mercy, to put Pharao in mind that he take me out of this prison:

text.NRSV: For in fact I was stolen out of the land of the Hebrews; and here also I have done nothing that they should have put me into the dungeon.”
text.Rotherham: For I was, stolen, out of the land of the Hebrews,—and, even here, had I done nothing, that they should have put me in the dungeon,
text.KJV: For indeed I was stollen away out of the land of the Hebrewes: and here also haue I done nothing, that they should put me into the dungeon.
text.NABRE: The truth is that I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and I have not done anything here that they should have put me into a dungeon.”
text.DRB: because I was taken away by stealth, out of the land of the Hebrews, and here an innocent was I cast into the lake.

text.NRSV: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was favorable, he said to Joseph, “I also had a dream: there were three cake baskets on my head,
text.Rotherham: Then the chief of the bakers, seeing that he had well interpreted, said unto Joseph, I, too, was in my dream, when lo! three wicker baskets of fine bread were on my head;
text.KJV: When the chiefe Baker saw, that the interpretation was good, he said vnto Ioseph, I also was in my dreame, and behold, I had three white baskets on my head.
text.NABRE: When the chief baker saw that Joseph had given a favorable interpretation, he said to him: “I too had a dream. In it I had three bread baskets on my head;
text.DRB: The master of the bakers seeing that he had wisely resolved the dream, he said: And I also saw a dream, that I had three baskets of meal upon my head:

text.NRSV: and in the uppermost basket there were all sorts of baked food for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating it out of the basket on my head.”
text.Rotherham: and, in the uppermost basket, was some of every kind of food for Pharaoh that a baker could make,—but, the birds, kept eating them out of the basket from off my head.
text.KJV: And in the vppermost basket there was of all maner of bake-meats for Pharaoh, and the birds did eat them out of the basket vpon my head.
text.NABRE: in the top one were all kinds of bakery products for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them out of the basket on my head.”
text.DRB: and that in one basket that was the higher, I carried all meats that are made by the art of baking, and that the birds did eat out of it.

text.NRSV: And Joseph answered, “This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days;
text.Rotherham: And Joseph responded and said, This, is the interpretation thereof, The three baskets, are, three days:
text.KJV: And Ioseph answered, and said, This is the interpretation thereof: the three baskets are three dayes:
text.NABRE: Joseph said to him in reply: “This is its interpretation. The three baskets are three days;
text.DRB: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three baskets, are yet three days:

text.NRSV: within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head—from you!—and hang you on a pole; and the birds will eat the flesh from you.”
text.Rotherham: In three days more, will Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and hang thee upon a tree, and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee.
text.KJV: Yet within three dayes shall Pharaoh lift vp thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree, and the birds shall eate thy flesh from off thee.
text.NABRE: within three days Pharaoh will single you out and will impale you on a stake, and the birds will be eating your flesh.”
text.DRB: after the which Pharao will take thy head from thee, and hang thee on the cross, and the fowls shall tear thy flesh.

text.NRSV: On the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, he made a feast for all his servants, and lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker among his servants.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass on the third day the birthday of Pharaoh, that he made a banquet for all his servants, and uplifted the head of the chief of the butlers and the head of the chief of the bakers in the midst of his servants;
text.KJV: ¶ And it came to passe the third day, which was Pharaohs birth day, that hee made a feast vnto all his seruaunts: and he lifted vp by the head of the chiefe Butler, and of the chiefe Baker among his seruants.
text.NABRE: And so on the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, when he gave a banquet to all his servants, he singled out the chief cupbearer and chief baker in the midst of his servants.
text.DRB: The third day after this was the birth-day of Pharao: who making a great feast to his servants, at the banquet he remembered the master of the cup-bearers, and the chief of the bakers.

text.NRSV: He restored the chief cupbearer to his cupbearing, and he placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand;
text.Rotherham: and restored the chief of the butlers to his butlership, so that he set the cup upon the palm of Pharaoh;
text.KJV: And he restored the chiefe Butler vnto his Butlership againe, and hee gaue the cup into Pharaohs hand.
text.NABRE: He restored the chief cupbearer to his office, so that he again handed the cup to Pharaoh;
text.DRB: And he restored the one into his place, to reach him the cup,

text.NRSV: but the chief baker he hanged, just as Joseph had interpreted to them.
text.Rotherham: but, the chief of the bakers, he hanged,—as, Joseph, had interpreted unto them.
text.KJV: But he hanged the chiefe Baker, as Ioseph had interpreted to them.
text.NABRE: but the chief baker he impaled—just as Joseph had told them in his interpretation.
text.DRB: the other he hanged on a gibbet, that the truth of the interpreter might be approved.

text.NRSV: Yet the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him.
text.Rotherham: Yet the chief of the butlers made no mention of Joseph but did forget him.
text.KJV: Yet did not the chiefe Butler remember Ioseph, but forgate him.
text.NABRE: Yet the chief cupbearer did not think of Joseph; he forgot him.
text.DRB: And yet notwithstanding the chief of the cup-bearers, prosperous things succeeding, forgat his interpreter.

text.NRSV: After two whole years, Pharaoh dreamed that he was standing by the Nile,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass at the end of two years of days, that, Pharaoh, was dreaming, when lo! he was standing by the river (Nile);
text.KJV: And it came to passe at the end of two ful yeeres, that Pharaoh dreamed: and beholde, hee stood by the riuer.
text.NABRE: After a lapse of two years, Pharaoh had a dream. He was standing by the Nile,
text.DRB: After two years Pharao saw a dream. He thought he stood upon a river,

text.NRSV: and there came up out of the Nile seven sleek and fat cows, and they grazed in the reed grass.
text.Rotherham: and lo! from the river, were coming up seven heifers, comely in appearance and fat in flesh,—and they fed among the rushes.
text.KJV: And behold, there came vp out of the riuer seuen well fauoured kine, and fat fleshed, and they fed in a medow.
text.NABRE: when up out of the Nile came seven cows, fine-looking and fat; they grazed in the reed grass.
text.DRB: out of the which came up seven kine, fair and fat exceedingly: and they fed in marish places.

text.NRSV: Then seven other cows, ugly and thin, came up out of the Nile after them, and stood by the other cows on the bank of the Nile.
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven heifers more coming up after them out of the river, uncomely in appearance and lean in flesh,—and they came and stood beside the heifers, by the lip of the river.
text.KJV: And behold, seuen other kine came vp after them out of the riuer, ill fauoured and leane fleshed, and stood by the other kine, vpon the brinke of the riuer.
text.NABRE: Behind them seven other cows, poor-looking and gaunt, came up out of the Nile; and standing on the bank of the Nile beside the others,
text.DRB: Other seven also came up out of the river, foul, and carrion lean: and they fed on the very bank of the river, in green places:

text.NRSV: The ugly and thin cows ate up the seven sleek and fat cows. And Pharaoh awoke.
text.Rotherham: Then did the heifers that were uncomely in appearance, and lean in flesh, eat up, the seven heifers that were comely in appearance and fat. So Pharaoh awoke.
text.KJV: And the ill fauoured and leane fleshed kine, did eate vp the seuen well fauoured and fat kine: So Pharaoh awoke.
text.NABRE: the poor-looking, gaunt cows devoured the seven fine-looking, fat cows. Then Pharaoh woke up.
text.DRB: and they devoured them that had the marvelous beauty and good state of bodies. Pharao after he waked,

text.NRSV: Then he fell asleep and dreamed a second time; seven ears of grain, plump and good, were growing on one stalk.
text.Rotherham: And he fell asleep, and dreamed a second time, when lo! seven ears, coming up on one stalk fat and good;
text.KJV: And hee slept and dreamed the second time: and beholde, seuen eares of corne came vp vpon one stalke, ranke and good.
text.NABRE: He fell asleep again and had another dream. He saw seven ears of grain, fat and healthy, growing on a single stalk.
text.DRB: slept again, and saw another dream: Seven ears of corn grew forth upon one stalk full and fair:

text.NRSV: Then seven ears, thin and blighted by the east wind, sprouted after them.
text.Rotherham: and lo! seven ears, lean and shrivelled by an east wind, coming up after them.
text.KJV: And beholde, seuen thinne eares and blasted with the Eastwind, sprang vp after them.
text.NABRE: Behind them sprouted seven ears of grain, thin and scorched by the east wind;
text.DRB: there sprang also other ears as many, thin and blasted with adustion,

text.NRSV: The thin ears swallowed up the seven plump and full ears. Pharaoh awoke, and it was a dream.
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean ears swallow up the seven fat and full ears. So Pharaoh awoke and lo! it was a dream.
text.KJV: And the seuen thinne eares deuoured the seuen ranke and full eares: and Pharaoh awoke, and behold, it was a dreame.
text.NABRE: and the thin ears swallowed up the seven fat, healthy ears. Then Pharaoh woke up—it was a dream!
text.DRB: devouring all the beauty of the former. Pharao awaking up after his rest,

text.NRSV: In the morning his spirit was troubled; so he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt and all its wise men. Pharaoh told them his dreams, but there was no one who could interpret them to Pharaoh.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the morning, that his spirit became restless, so he sent and called for all the sacred scribes of Egypt and all her wise men,—and Pharaoh related to them his dreams, but there was no one that could interpret them. to Pharaoh.
text.KJV: And it came to passe in the morning, that his spirit was troubled, and he sent and called for all the Magicians of Egypt, and all the wise men thereof: and Pharaoh tolde them his dreame; but there was none that could interprete them vnto Pharaoh.
text.NABRE: Next morning his mind was agitated. So Pharaoh had all the magicians and sages of Egypt summoned and recounted his dream to them; but there was no one to interpret it for him.
text.DRB: and when morning was come being frighted with fear, he sent to all the interpreters of Ægypt, and to all the wise men: and they being called for, told them his dream, neither was there any that could interpret it.

text.NRSV: Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh, “I remember my faults today.
text.Rotherham: Then spake the chief of the butlers with Pharaoh saying, My faults, would, I, mention this day.
text.KJV: ¶ Then spake the chiefe Butler vnto Pharaoh, saying, I doe remember my faults this day.
text.NABRE: Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh: “Now I remember my negligence!
text.DRB: Then at length the master of the cup-bearers remembering himself, said: I confess my sin:

text.NRSV: Once Pharaoh was angry with his servants, and put me and the chief baker in custody in the house of the captain of the guard.
text.Rotherham: Pharaoh, was wroth with his servants,—and put them in the ward of the house of the chief of the royal executioners, me, and the chief of the bakers.
text.KJV: Pharaoh was wroth with his seruants, and put mee in warde, in the captaine of the guards house, both mee, and the chiefe Baker.
text.NABRE: Once, when Pharaoh was angry with his servants, he put me and the chief baker in custody in the house of the chief steward.
text.DRB: The king being angry with his servants, commanded me and the chief of the bakers to be cast into the prison of the Captain of the soldiers:

text.NRSV: We dreamed on the same night, he and I, each having a dream with its own meaning.
text.Rotherham: Then must we needs dream, a dream in one night, I and he,—each man, according to the interpretation of his dream, did we dream.
text.KJV: And we dreamed a dreame in one night, I and he: we dreamed each man according to the interpretation of his dreame.
text.NABRE: Later, we both had dreams on the same night, and each of our dreams had its own meaning.
text.DRB: where in one night both of us saw a dream portending things to come.

text.NRSV: A young Hebrew was there with us, a servant of the captain of the guard. When we told him, he interpreted our dreams to us, giving an interpretation to each according to his dream.
text.Rotherham: And, there with us, was a young man, a Hebrew servant to the chief of the royal executioners, and we related to him, and he interpreted to us our dreams,—to each man—according to his dream, did he interpret,
text.KJV: And there was there with vs a yong man an Hebrew, seruant to the captaine of the guard: and wee told him, and he interpreted to vs our dreames, to each man according to his dreame, he did interpret.
text.NABRE: There was a Hebrew youth with us, a slave of the chief steward; and when we told him our dreams, he interpreted them for us and explained for each of us the meaning of his dream.
text.DRB: There was there a young man an Hebrew, servant to the same Captain of the soldiers, to whom telling our dreams,

text.NRSV: As he interpreted to us, so it turned out; I was restored to my office, and the baker was hanged.”
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass as he interpreted to us, so, it happened,—me, he restored to mine office but him, he hanged.
text.KJV: And it came to passe, as he interpreted to vs, so it was; mee he restored vnto mine office, and him he hanged.
text.NABRE: Things turned out just as he had told us: I was restored to my post, but the other man was impaled.”
text.DRB: we heard whatsoever afterward the event of the thing proved to be so. For I was restored to my office, and he was hanged upon a gibbet.

text.NRSV: Then Pharaoh sent for Joseph, and he was hurriedly brought out of the dungeon. When he had shaved himself and changed his clothes, he came in before Pharaoh.
text.Rotherham: Then sent Pharaoh and summoned Joseph, and they hastened him out of the dungeon,—so he shaved himself and changed his garments, and came in unto Pharaoh.
text.KJV: ¶ Then Pharaoh sent and called Ioseph, and they brought him hastily out of the dungeon: And he shaued himselfe, and changed his raiment, and came in vnto Pharaoh.
text.NABRE: Pharaoh therefore had Joseph summoned, and they hurriedly brought him from the dungeon. After he shaved and changed his clothes, he came to Pharaoh.
text.DRB: Forthwith at the king's commandment, Joseph being brought out of the prison they polled him: and changing his apparel, brought him unto him.

text.NRSV: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I have had a dream, and there is no one who can interpret it. I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream you can interpret it.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, A dream, have I dreamed, but none can interpret it,—but, I, have heard say concerning thee, that on hearing a dream, thou canst interpret it.
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto Ioseph, I haue dreamed a dreame, and there is none that can interpret it: and I haue heard say of thee, that thou canst vnderstand a dreame, to interpret it.
text.NABRE: Pharaoh then said to Joseph: “I had a dream but there was no one to interpret it. But I hear it said of you, ‘If he hears a dream he can interpret it.’”
text.DRB: To whom he said: I have seen dreams, and there is not any that can expound them; which I have heard, thou dost most wisely interpret.

text.NRSV: Joseph answered Pharaoh, “It is not I; God will give Pharaoh a favorable answer.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph answered Pharaoh saying, Not to me, doth it pertain! May, God grant in answer, the prosperity of Pharaoh!
text.KJV: And Ioseph answered Pharaoh, saying; It is not in me: God shall giue Pharaoh an answere of peace.
text.NABRE: “It is not I,” Joseph replied to Pharaoh, “but God who will respond for the well-being of Pharaoh.”
text.DRB: Joseph answered: Without me, God shall answer prosperous things to Pharao.

text.NRSV: Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “In my dream I was standing on the banks of the Nile;
text.Rotherham: Then spake Pharaoh unto Joseph,—In my dream, there was I, standing on the lip of the river;
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto Ioseph; In my dreame, behold, I stood vpon the banke of the riuer.
text.NABRE: Then Pharaoh said to Joseph: “In my dream, I was standing on the bank of the Nile,
text.DRB: Pharao therefore told that he had see: Me thought I stood upon the bank of the river,

text.NRSV: and seven cows, fat and sleek, came up out of the Nile and fed in the reed grass.
text.Rotherham: When lo! out of the river, were coming up seven heifers, fat in flesh and comely in form,—and they fed among the rushes.
text.KJV: And behold, there came vp out of the riuer seuen kine, fat fleshed and well fauoured, and they fed in a medow.
text.NABRE: when up from the Nile came seven cows, fat and well-formed; they grazed in the reed grass.
text.DRB: and seven kine came up out of the bank of the river, exceeding fair, and full of flesh: which grazed on green places in a marish pasture.

text.NRSV: Then seven other cows came up after them, poor, very ugly, and thin. Never had I seen such ugly ones in all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven other heifers, coming up after them, poor and very uncomely in form and lean in flesh,—I had never seen such in all the land of Egypt, for uncomeliness.
text.KJV: And behold, seuen other kine came vp after them, poore and very ill fauoured, and leane fleshed, such as I neuer saw in all the land of Egypt for badnes.
text.NABRE: Behind them came seven other cows, scrawny, most ill-formed and gaunt. Never have I seen such bad specimens as these in all the land of Egypt!
text.DRB: And behold there followed these other seven kine, so passing ill favoured and lean, that I never saw the like in the land of Ægypt;

text.NRSV: The thin and ugly cows ate up the first seven fat cows,
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean and uncomely heifers eat up the first seven fat heifers;
text.KJV: And the leane, & the ill fauoured kine, did eate vp the first seuen fat kine.
text.NABRE: The gaunt, bad cows devoured the first seven fat cows.
text.DRB: which having devoured and consumed the former,

text.NRSV: but when they had eaten them no one would have known that they had done so, for they were still as ugly as before. Then I awoke.
text.Rotherham: and they passed into their stomach yet could it not be known that they had passed into them, their appearance, being uncomely, as at the beginning. So I awoke.
text.KJV: And when they had eaten them vp, it could not bee knowen that they had eaten them, but they were still ill fauoured, as at the beginning: So I awoke.
text.NABRE: But when they had consumed them, no one could tell that they had done so, because they looked as bad as before. Then I woke up.
text.DRB: gave no token of their fullness: but with the like leanness & deformity looked heavily. Awaking, and fallen again into a deep sleep,

text.NRSV: I fell asleep a second time and I saw in my dream seven ears of grain, full and good, growing on one stalk,
text.Rotherham: Then looked I in my dream,—And lo! seven ears, coming up on one stalk, full and good;
text.KJV: And I saw in my dreame, and behold, seuen eares came vp in one stalke, full and good.
text.NABRE: In another dream I saw seven ears of grain, full and healthy, growing on a single stalk.
text.DRB: Isaw a dream: Seven ears of corn grew forth upon one stalk, full and very fair.

text.NRSV: and seven ears, withered, thin, and blighted by the east wind, sprouting after them;
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven ears, withered lean shrivelled by an east wind growing up after them.
text.KJV: And behold, seuen eares withered, thin & blasted with the East wind, sprung vp after them.
text.NABRE: Behind them sprouted seven ears of grain, shriveled and thin and scorched by the east wind;
text.DRB: Other seven also thin and blasted with adustion, sprang of the stalk,

text.NRSV: and the thin ears swallowed up the seven good ears. But when I told it to the magicians, there was no one who could explain it to me.”
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean ears swallow up, the seven good ears. So I told [these things] unto the sacred scribes, but there was none that could explain [them] to me.
text.KJV: And the thin eares deuoured the seuen good eares: and I told this vnto the magicians, but there was none that could declare it to me.
text.NABRE: and the seven thin ears swallowed up the seven healthy ears. I have spoken to the magicians, but there is no one to explain it to me.”
text.DRB: which devoured the beauty of the former: I told the dream to the conjecturers, and there is no man that can declare it.

text.NRSV: Then Joseph said to Pharaoh, “Pharaoh’s dreams are one and the same; God has revealed to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joseph unto Pharaoh, The dream of Pharaoh, is, one. What God is about to do hath he announced to Pharaoh.
text.KJV: ¶ And Ioseph said vnto Pharaoh, the dreame of Pharaoh is one; God hath shewed Pharaoh what he is about to doe.
text.NABRE: Joseph said to Pharaoh: “Pharaoh’s dreams have the same meaning. God has made known to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.DRB: Joseph answered: The king's dream is one: God hath shewed to Pharao the things that he will do.

text.NRSV: The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good ears are seven years; the dreams are one.
text.Rotherham: The seven good heifers, are, seven years, and, the seven good ears are, seven years,—the dream, is, one.
text.KJV: The seuen good kine are seuen yeeres: and the seuen good eares are seuen yeeres: the dreame is one.
text.NABRE: The seven healthy cows are seven years, and the seven healthy ears are seven years—the same in each dream.
text.DRB: The seven fair kine, and the seven full ears, be seven years of plentifulness and both contain the self-same meaning of the dream.

text.NRSV: The seven lean and ugly cows that came up after them are seven years, as are the seven empty ears blighted by the east wind. They are seven years of famine.
text.Rotherham: And the seven lean and uncomely heifers that were coming up after them, are seven years, and the seven lean ears, shrivelled by an east wind, will turn out to be—seven years of famine.
text.KJV: And the seuen thin and ill fauoured kine that came vp after them, are seuen yeeres: and the seuen emptie eares blasted with the East wind, shall bee seuen yeeres of famine.
text.NABRE: The seven thin, bad cows that came up after them are seven years, as are the seven thin ears scorched by the east wind; they are seven years of famine.
text.DRB: Also the seven lean and thin kine, that came up after them, and the seven thin ears, and blasted with the burning wind, are seven years of famine to come.

text.NRSV: It is as I told Pharaoh; God has shown to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.Rotherham: The very word that I spake unto Pharaoh, what, God, is about to do, hath he showed unto Pharaoh.
text.KJV: This is the thing which I haue spoken vnto Pharaoh: what God is about to doe, he sheweth vnto Pharaoh.
text.NABRE: Things are just as I told Pharaoh: God has revealed to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.DRB: Which shall be fulfilled in this order.

text.NRSV: There will come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Lo! seven years, coming in,—of great plenty, in all the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: Behold, there come seuen yeeres of great plentie, throughout all the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Seven years of great abundance are now coming throughout the land of Egypt;
text.DRB: Behold there shall come seven years of great fertility in the whole Land of Ægypt:

text.NRSV: After them there will arise seven years of famine, and all the plenty will be forgotten in the land of Egypt; the famine will consume the land.
text.Rotherham: Then shall arise seven years of famine, after them, so shall be forgotten all the plenty in the land of Egypt,—and the famine shall consume the land;
text.KJV: And there shall arise after them, seuen yeeres of famine, and all the plentie shall be forgotten in the land of Egypt: and the famine shall consume the land.
text.NABRE: but seven years of famine will rise up after them, when all the abundance will be forgotten in the land of Egypt. When the famine has exhausted the land,
text.DRB: after which shall follow other seven years of so great sterility, that all the abundance before shall be forgotten: for the famine shall consume all the land,

text.NRSV: The plenty will no longer be known in the land because of the famine that will follow, for it will be very grievous.
text.Rotherham: neither shall the plenty in the land be discernible, because of that famine coming after,—for it shall be, very severe.
text.KJV: And the plentie shal not be knowen in the land, by reason of that famine following: for it shalbe very grieuous.
text.NABRE: no trace of the abundance will be found in the land because of the famine that follows it, for it will be very severe.
text.DRB: and the greatness of the scarcity shall destroy the greatness of the plenty.

text.NRSV: And the doubling of Pharaoh’s dream means that the thing is fixed by God, and God will shortly bring it about.
text.Rotherham: And for that there was a repeating of the dream unto Pharaoh, twice, it is because the thing, is established, from God, and God is hastening to do it.
text.KJV: And for that the dreame was doubled vnto Pharaoh twice, it is because the thing is established by God: and God will shortly bring it to passe.
text.NABRE: That Pharaoh had the same dream twice means that the matter has been confirmed by God and that God will soon bring it about.
text.DRB: And in that thou didst see the second time a dream pertaining to the same thing, it is a token of the certainty for that the word of God shall come to pass, and be fulfilled speedily.

text.NRSV: Now therefore let Pharaoh select a man who is discerning and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise,—and set him over the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: Now therfore let Pharaoh looke out a man discreet and wise, and set him ouer the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: “Therefore, let Pharaoh seek out a discerning and wise man and put him in charge of the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: Now therefore let the king provide a wise man and industrious, & make him Ruler over the Land of Ægypt:

text.NRSV: Let Pharaoh proceed to appoint overseers over the land, and take one-fifth of the produce of the land of Egypt during the seven plenteous years.
text.Rotherham: Let Pharaoh do this, that he may appoint overseers over the land,—so shall he take up a fifth of the land of Egypt, during the seven years of plenty.
text.KJV: Let Pharaoh doe this, and let him appoint officers ouer the land, & take vp the fift part of the land of Egypt, in the seuen plenteous yeeres.
text.NABRE: Let Pharaoh act and appoint overseers for the land to organize it during the seven years of abundance.
text.DRB: that he may appoint overseers over all countries: and gather into barns the fifth part of the fruits, during the seven years of the fertility,

text.NRSV: Let them gather all the food of these good years that are coming, and lay up grain under the authority of Pharaoh for food in the cities, and let them keep it.
text.Rotherham: And let them gather up all the food of these seven good years that are coming in,—and let them heap up corn under the hand of Pharaoh as food in cities so shall they keep it.
text.KJV: And let them gather all the food of those good yeeres that come, and lay vp corne vnder the hand of Pharaoh, and let them keepe food in the cities.
text.NABRE: They should collect all the food of these coming good years, gathering the grain under Pharaoh’s authority, for food in the cities, and they should guard it.
text.DRB: that now presently shall ensue: and let all the corn be laid up under Pharao's hands, and let it be reserved in the cities.

text.NRSV: That food shall be a reserve for the land against the seven years of famine that are to befall the land of Egypt, so that the land may not perish through the famine.”
text.Rotherham: So shall the food become a store for the land, for the seven years of famine which shall come about in the land of Egypt,—and the land shall not be cut off in the famine.
text.KJV: And that food shall be for store to the land, against the seuen yeeres of famine, which shall bee in the land of Egypt, that the land perish not through the famine.
text.NABRE: This food will serve as a reserve for the country against the seven years of famine that will occur in the land of Egypt, so that the land may not perish in the famine.”
text.DRB: And let it be in a readiness against the famine of seven years to come, which shall oppress Ægypt, and the land shall not be consumed with scarcity.

text.NRSV: The proposal pleased Pharaoh and all his servants.
text.Rotherham: And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh,—and in the eyes of all his servants;
text.KJV: ¶ And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his seruants.
text.NABRE: This advice pleased Pharaoh and all his servants.
text.DRB: The counsel pleased Pharao, and all his servants:

text.NRSV: Pharaoh said to his servants, “Can we find anyone else like this—one in whom is the spirit of God?”
text.Rotherham: and Pharaoh said unto his servants,—Can we find such a one, a man in whom is the Spirit of God?
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto his seruants, Can we find such a one, as this is, a man in whom the spirit of God is?
text.NABRE: “Could we find another like him,” Pharaoh asked his servants, “a man so endowed with the spirit of God?”
text.DRB: and he spake to them: Can we find such another man, that is full of the spirit of God?

text.NRSV: So Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has shown you all this, there is no one so discerning and wise as you.
text.Rotherham: So Pharaoh said unto Joseph, After God hath made known unto thee all this, there is no one discreet and wise like thee.
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto Ioseph, Forasmuch as God hath shewed thee all this, there is none so discreete and wise, as thou art:
text.NABRE: So Pharaoh said to Joseph: “Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one as discerning and wise as you are.
text.DRB: He said therefore to Joseph: Because God hath shewed thee all things that thou hast spoken, can I find a wiser and one like unto thee?

text.NRSV: You shall be over my house, and all my people shall order themselves as you command; only with regard to the throne will I be greater than you.”
text.Rotherham: Thou, shalt be over my house, and on thy mouth, shall all my people kiss,—only as to the throne, will I be greater than thou.
text.KJV: Thou shalt be ouer my house, and according vnto thy word shall all my people be ruled: only in the throne will I be greater then thou.
text.NABRE: You shall be in charge of my household, and all my people will obey your command. Only in respect to the throne will I outrank you.”
text.DRB: Thou shalt be over my house, & at the commandment of thy mouth all the people shall obey: only in the throne of the Kingdom I will go before thee.

text.NRSV: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “See, I have set you over all the land of Egypt.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph,—See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto Ioseph, See, I haue set thee ouer all the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Look, I put you in charge of the whole land of Egypt.”
text.DRB: And again Pharao said to Joseph: Behold, I have appointed thee over the whole land of Ægypt.

text.NRSV: Removing his signet ring from his hand, Pharaoh put it on Joseph’s hand; he arrayed him in garments of fine linen, and put a gold chain around his neck.
text.Rotherham: So Pharaoh took his ring from off his hand and put it upon Joseph’s hand,—and clothed him in garments of fine linen, and put the chain of gold upon his neck;
text.KJV: And Pharaoh tooke off his ring from his hand, & put it vpon Iosephs hand, and arayed him in vestures of fine linnen, and put a gold chaine about his necke.
text.NABRE: With that, Pharaoh took off his signet ring and put it on Joseph’s finger. He dressed him in robes of fine linen and put a gold chain around his neck.
text.DRB: And he took his ring from his own hand, and gave it into his hand: and he put upon him a silk robe, and put a chain of gold about his neck.

text.NRSV: He had him ride in the chariot of his second-in-command; and they cried out in front of him, “Bow the knee!” Thus he set him over all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: and made him ride in the second chariot which belonged to him, and they cried out before him. Bow the knee! thus setting him over all the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: And he made him to ride in the second charet which he had: and they cried before him, Bow the knee: and he made him ruler ouer all the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: He then had him ride in his second chariot, and they shouted “Abrek!” before him. Thus was Joseph installed over the whole land of Egypt.
text.DRB: And he made him go up into his second chariot, the crier proclaiming that all should bow their knee before him, and that they should know he was made Governor over the whole Land of Ægypt.

text.NRSV: Moreover Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I am Pharaoh, and without your consent no one shall lift up hand or foot in all the land of Egypt.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph. I, am Pharaoh, Without thee, therefore shall no man raise his hand or his foot in all the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: And Pharaoh said vnto Ioseph, I am Pharaoh, and without thee shall no man lift vp his hand or foote, in all the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: “I am Pharaoh,” he told Joseph, “but without your approval no one shall lift hand or foot in all the land of Egypt.”
text.DRB: And the king said to Joseph: I am Pharao: without thy commandment no man shall move hand or foot in all the land of Ægypt.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh gave Joseph the name Zaphenath-paneah; and he gave him Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, as his wife. Thus Joseph gained authority over the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh called Joseph’s name Zaphenath-paneah, and gave him Asenath daughter of Poti-phera priest of On, to wife,—and Joseph went forth over the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: And Pharaoh called Iosephs name, Zaphnath-Paaneah, and he gaue him to wife Asenath the daughter of Poti-pherah, priest of On: and Ioseph went out ouer all the lande of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Pharaoh also bestowed the name of Zaphenath-paneah on Joseph, and he gave him in marriage Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, priest of Heliopolis. And Joseph went out over the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: And he turned his name, & called him in the Ægyptian tongue the Saviour of the world. And he gave him to wife Aseneth the daughter of Putiphar Priest of Heliopolis. Joseph therefore went forth to the land of Ægypt

text.NRSV: Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went through all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, Joseph, was thirty years old, when he took his station, before Pharaoh king of Egypt. So Joseph went forth from before Pharaoh, and passed along through all the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: (¶ And Ioseph was thirtie yeeres old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt) and Ioseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went thorowout all the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh, king of Egypt. After Joseph left Pharaoh, he went throughout the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: (and he was thirty years old when he stood in the sight of king Pharao) and did circuit all the countries of Ægypt.

text.NRSV: During the seven plenteous years the earth produced abundantly.
text.Rotherham: And the land produced, in the seven years of plenty, by handfuls,
text.KJV: And in the seuen plenteous yeres the earth brought forth by handfuls.
text.NABRE: During the seven years of plenty, when the land produced abundant crops,
text.DRB: And the fertility of the seven years came: and the corn being bound up into sheaves was gathered into the barns of Ægypt.

text.NRSV: He gathered up all the food of the seven years when there was plenty in the land of Egypt, and stored up food in the cities; he stored up in every city the food from the fields around it.
text.Rotherham: And he gathered up all the food of the seven years in which there was plenty in the land of Egypt, and laid up food in cities—the food of the fields of the city. which were round about it, laid he up within it.
text.KJV: And he gathered vp all the foode of the seuen yeeres, which were in the land of Egypt, and laid vp the foode in the cities: the foode of the field which was round about euery citie, laid he vp in the same.
text.NABRE: he collected all the food of these years of plenty that the land of Egypt was enjoying and stored it in the cities, placing in each city the crops of the fields around it.
text.DRB: All the abundance also of grain was laid up in every city.

text.NRSV: So Joseph stored up grain in such abundance—like the sand of the sea—that he stopped measuring it; it was beyond measure.
text.Rotherham: Thus did Joseph heap up corn like the sand of the sea making it exceeding abundant,—until one hath left off reckoning, because it cannot be reckoned.
text.KJV: And Ioseph gathered corne as the sand of the sea, very much, vntill he left numbring: for it was without number.
text.NABRE: Joseph collected grain like the sands of the sea, so much that at last he stopped measuring it, for it was beyond measure.
text.DRB: And there was so great abundance of wheat, that it became equal to the sand of the sea, and the plenty exceeded measure.

text.NRSV: Before the years of famine came, Joseph had two sons, whom Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him.
text.Rotherham: Now to Joseph, were born two sons, ere yet came in the year of famine,—whom Asenath daughter of Poti-phera priest of On, bare to him.
text.KJV: And vnto Ioseph were borne two sonnes, before the yeeres of famine came: which Asenath the daughter of Poti-pherah, Priest of On bare vnto him.
text.NABRE: Before the famine years set in, Joseph became the father of two sons, borne to him by Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of Heliopolis.
text.DRB: And there were born unto Joseph two sons before the famine came: whom Aseneth the daughter of Putiphar Priest of Heliopolis bare him.

text.NRSV: Joseph named the firstborn Manasseh, “For,” he said, “God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father’s house.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh,—For God hath made me forget all my trouble, and all the house of my father.
text.KJV: And Ioseph called the name of the first borne Manasseh: for God, said hee, hath made me forget all my toile, and all my fathers house.
text.NABRE: Joseph named his firstborn Manasseh, meaning, “God has made me forget entirely my troubles and my father’s house”;
text.DRB: And he called the name of the first-begotten Manasses, saying: God made me to forget all my labours, and my father's house.

text.NRSV: The second he named Ephraim, “For God has made me fruitful in the land of my misfortunes.”
text.Rotherham: And, the name of the second, called he Ephraim, For God hath made me fruitful in the land of my humiliation.
text.KJV: And the name of the second called he Ephraim: for God hath caused mee to be fruitfull in the land of my affliction.
text.NABRE: and the second he named Ephraim, meaning, “God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.”
text.DRB: The name also of the second he called Ephraim, saying: God hath made me to increase in the land of my poverty.

text.NRSV: The seven years of plenty that prevailed in the land of Egypt came to an end;
text.Rotherham: Then came to an end the seven years of the plenty,—which was in the land of Egypt;
text.KJV: ¶ And the seuen yeeres of plenteousnesse, that was in the land of Egypt, were ended.
text.NABRE: When the seven years of abundance enjoyed by the land of Egypt came to an end,
text.DRB: Therefore when the seven years of the plentifulness, that had been in Ægypt were passed:

text.NRSV: and the seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had said. There was famine in every country, but throughout the land of Egypt there was bread.
text.Rotherham: and the seven years of famine began to come in, according as Joseph had said,—and it came to pass that there was a famine in all the lands, but in all the land of Egypt, there was bread.
text.KJV: And the seuen yeeres of dearth beganne to come according as Ioseph had saide, and the dearth was in all lands: but in all the land of Egypt there was bread.
text.NABRE: the seven years of famine set in, just as Joseph had said. Although there was famine in all the other countries, food was available throughout the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: the seven years of scarcity began to come, which Joseph foretold: and in the whole world the famine prevailed, but in all the land of Ægypt there was bread.

text.NRSV: When all the land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread. Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians, “Go to Joseph; what he says to you, do.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was famine felt in all the land of Egypt, and the people made outcry; unto Pharaoh for bread,—and Pharaoh said to all Egypt,—Go ye unto Joseph, that which he saith to you, shall ye do.
text.KJV: And when all the land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread: and Pharaoh said vnto all the Egyptians, Goe vnto Ioseph: what he saith to you, doe.
text.NABRE: When all the land of Egypt became hungry and the people cried to Pharaoh for food, Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians: “Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you.”
text.DRB: The which being in hunger, the people cried to Pharao, desiring food. To whom he answered: Go ye to Joseph: & whatsoever he shall say to you, that do ye.

text.NRSV: And since the famine had spread over all the land, Joseph opened all the storehouses, and sold to the Egyptians, for the famine was severe in the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, the famine, was over all the face of the land,—so Joseph opened all [places] wherein it was and sold corn to the Egyptians, and the famine laid fast hold of the land of Egypt.
text.KJV: And the famine was ouer all the face of the earth; and Ioseph opened all the storehouses, and solde vnto the Egyptians: and the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt.
text.NABRE: When the famine had spread throughout the land, Joseph opened all the cities that had grain and rationed it to the Egyptians, since the famine had gripped the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: And the famine daily increased in all the land: and Joseph opened all the barns, and sold to the Ægyptians: for them also the famine had oppressed.

text.NRSV: Moreover, all the world came to Joseph in Egypt to buy grain, because the famine became severe throughout the world.
text.Rotherham: All the earth also, came in to Egypt to buy corn, unto Joseph,—because the famine had laid fast hold on all the earth.
text.KJV: And all countreys came into Egypt to Ioseph, for to buy corne, because that the famine was so sore in all lands.
text.NABRE: Indeed, the whole world came to Egypt to Joseph to buy grain, for famine had gripped the whole world.
text.DRB: And all Provinces came into Ægypt, to buy victuals, and to moderate the misery of the scarcity.

text.NRSV: When Jacob learned that there was grain in Egypt, he said to his sons, “Why do you keep looking at one another?
text.Rotherham: Then saw Jacob that there was corn in Egypt,—and Jacob said to his sons, Wherefore look ye one at another?
text.KJV: Now when Iacob saw that there was corne in Egypt, Iacob said vnto his sonnes, Why doe ye looke one vpon an other?
text.NABRE: When Jacob learned that grain rations were for sale in Egypt, he said to his sons: “Why do you keep looking at one another?”
text.DRB: And Jacob hearing that victuals were sold in Ægypt, he said to his sons: Why neglect ye?

text.NRSV: I have heard,” he said, “that there is grain in Egypt; go down and buy grain for us there, that we may live and not die.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, Lo I have heard that there is corn in Egypt,—Go down thither and buy corn for us from thence, that we may live and not die.
text.KJV: And hee said, Beholde, I haue heard that there is corne in Egypt: get you downe thither and buy for vs from thence, that we may liue, and not die.
text.NABRE: He went on, “I hear that grain is for sale in Egypt. Go down there and buy some for us, that we may stay alive and not die.”
text.DRB: I have heard that wheat is sold in Ægypt: Go ye down and buy us necessaries, that we may live and not be consumed with lack.

text.NRSV: So ten of Joseph’s brothers went down to buy grain in Egypt.
text.Rotherham: So Joseph’s ten brethren went down,—to buy corn from the Egyptians;
text.KJV: ¶ And Iosephs ten brethren went downe to buy corne in Egypt.
text.NABRE: So ten of Joseph’s brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.
text.DRB: Therefore the ten brethren of Joseph going down to buy corn in Ægypt,

text.NRSV: But Jacob did not send Joseph’s brother Benjamin with his brothers, for he feared that harm might come to him.
text.Rotherham: but, Benjamin, Joseph’s own brother, Jacob sent not with his brethren,—for he said, Lest there befall him any mischief.
text.KJV: But Beniamin Iosephs brother, Iacob sent not with his brethren: for he said, Lest peraduenture mischiefe befall him.
text.NABRE: But Jacob did not send Joseph’s brother Benjamin with his brothers, for he thought some disaster might befall him.
text.DRB: Benjamin being kept at home by Jacob, who said to his brethren: Lest perhaps he take any harm in the journey:

text.NRSV: Thus the sons of Israel were among the other people who came to buy grain, for the famine had reached the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: Thus came in the sons of Israel, to buy corn in the midst of them that came—for the famine was in the land of Canaan.
text.KJV: And the sonnes of Israel came to buy corne among those that came: for the famine was in the land of Canaan.
text.NABRE: And so the sons of Israel were among those who came to buy grain, since there was famine in the land of Canaan.
text.DRB: entered into the land of Ægypt with others that went to buy. For the famine was in the land of Chanaan.

text.NRSV: Now Joseph was governor over the land; it was he who sold to all the people of the land. And Joseph’s brothers came and bowed themselves before him with their faces to the ground.
text.Rotherham: Now, Joseph—he, it was, that was in power over the land, he, it was that was selling corn to all the people of the land,—so Joseph’s brethren came in, and bowed themselves down to him, with their faces to the earth.
text.KJV: And Ioseph was the gouernour ouer the land, and hee it was that sold to all the people of the land: and Iosephs brethren came, & bowed downe themselues before him, with their faces to the earth.
text.NABRE: Joseph, as governor of the country, was the one who sold grain to all the people of the land. When Joseph’s brothers came, they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground.
text.DRB: And Joseph was Prince in the land of Ægypt, and at his pleasure corn was sold to the people. And when his brethren had adored him,

text.NRSV: When Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them, but he treated them like strangers and spoke harshly to them. “Where do you come from?” he said. They said, “From the land of Canaan, to buy food.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph beheld his brethren and recognised them,—and made himself a stranger unto them and spake with them harsh things and said unto them—Whence have ye come in? And they said, From the land of Canaan, to buy food.
text.KJV: And Ioseph saw his brethren, and he knew them, but made himselfe strange vnto them, and spake roughly vnto them; and hee saide vnto them, Whence come ye? And they said, From the land of Canaan, to buy food.
text.NABRE: He recognized them as soon as he saw them. But he concealed his own identity from them and spoke harshly to them. “Where do you come from?” he asked them. They answered, “From the land of Canaan, to buy food.”
text.DRB: and he knew them, he spake as it were to strangers somewhat roughly, asking them: From whence came you? Who answered: From the Land of Chanaan, that we may buy necessaries to live.

text.NRSV: Although Joseph had recognized his brothers, they did not recognize him.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph recognised his brethren,—whereas they, recognised him not.
text.KJV: And Ioseph knew his brethren, but they knew not him.
text.NABRE: When Joseph recognized his brothers, although they did not recognize him,
text.DRB: And yet himself knowing his brethren, was not known of them.

text.NRSV: Joseph also remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them. He said to them, “You are spies; you have come to see the nakedness of the land!”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph remembered the dreams which he had dreamed regarding them,—so he said unto them—Spies, are, ye! to see the nakedness of the land, have ye come in!
text.KJV: And Ioseph remembred the dreames which hee dreamed of them, and said vnto them, Ye are spies: to see the nakednes of the land you are come.
text.NABRE: he was reminded of the dreams he had about them. He said to them: “You are spies. You have come to see the weak points of the land.”
text.DRB: And remembering the dreams, which sometimes he had seen, he said to them: You are spies: to view the weaker parts of the land you are come.

text.NRSV: They said to him, “No, my lord; your servants have come to buy food.
text.Rotherham: And they said unto him, Nay, my lord,—but, thy servants, are come to buy food.
text.KJV: And they said vnto him, Nay, my lord, but to buy food are thy seruants come.
text.NABRE: “No, my lord,” they replied. “On the contrary, your servants have come to buy food.
text.DRB: Who said: It is not so, my Lord, but thy servants are come to buy victuals.

text.NRSV: We are all sons of one man; we are honest men; your servants have never been spies.”
text.Rotherham: All of us, are, sons of one man,—honest men, are we: thy servants are not, spies.
text.KJV: We are all one mans sonnes, we are true men: thy seruants are no spies.
text.NABRE: All of us are sons of the same man. We are honest men; your servants have never been spies.”
text.DRB: We are all the sons of one man: we are come as men of peace, neither do thy servants go about any evil.

text.NRSV: But he said to them, “No, you have come to see the nakedness of the land!”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him,—Nay! but, the nakedness of the land, are ye come in to see.
text.KJV: And he said vnto them, Nay: but to see the nakednesse of the land, you are come.
text.NABRE: But he answered them: “Not so! It is the weak points of the land that you have come to see.”
text.DRB: To whom he answered: It is otherwise: you came to consider the undefensed parts of this land.

text.NRSV: They said, “We, your servants, are twelve brothers, the sons of a certain man in the land of Canaan; the youngest, however, is now with our father, and one is no more.”
text.Rotherham: And they said: We, thy twelve servants, are, brethren, sons of one man in the and of Canaan,—and lo! the youngest: is with our father this day, and, the one, is not!
text.KJV: And they said, Thy seruants are twelue brethren, the sonnes of one man in the land of Canaan: and behold, the yongest is this day with our father, and one is not.
text.NABRE: “We your servants,” they said, “are twelve brothers, sons of a certain man in Canaan; but the youngest one is at present with our father, and the other one is no more.”
text.DRB: But they: We thy servants, say they, are twelve brethren, the sons of one man in the Land of Chanaan: the youngest is with our father, the other is not living.

text.NRSV: But Joseph said to them, “It is just as I have said to you; you are spies!
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said unto them,—The very thing, that I spake unto you, saying—Spies, are, ye.
text.KJV: And Ioseph said vnto them, That is it that I spake vnto you, saying, Ye are spies.
text.NABRE: “It is just as I said,” Joseph persisted; “you are spies.
text.DRB: This is it, quoth he, that I said: You are spies.

text.NRSV: Here is how you shall be tested: as Pharaoh lives, you shall not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here!
text.Rotherham: Hereby, shall ye be proved,—By the life of Pharaoh, ye shall not go forth from hence, save only by the coming in of your youngest brother hither.
text.KJV: Hereby ye shall be proued: by the life of Pharaoh ye shall not goe foorth hence, except your yongest brother come hither.
text.NABRE: This is how you shall be tested: I swear by the life of Pharaoh that you shall not leave here unless your youngest brother comes here.
text.DRB: Now presently I will take a trial of you: by the health of Pharao you shall not depart hence, until your youngest brother do come,

text.NRSV: Let one of you go and bring your brother, while the rest of you remain in prison, in order that your words may be tested, whether there is truth in you; or else, as Pharaoh lives, surely you are spies.”
text.Rotherham: Send—from among you—one that he may fetch your brother, but be, ye, held as prisoners, that your words may be proved whether, truth, is with you,—and, if not, by the life of Pharaoh, surely, spies, ye are.
text.KJV: Send one of you, and let him fetch your brother, and ye shalbe kept in prison, that your wordes may be proued, whether there be any trueth in you: or els by the life of Pharaoh surely ye are spies.
text.NABRE: So send one of your number to get your brother, while the rest of you stay here under arrest. Thus will your words be tested for their truth; if they are untrue, as Pharaoh lives, you are spies!”
text.DRB: Send you one of you to bring him: and you shall be in prison till the things be proved which you have said, whether they be true or false: otherwise by the health of Pharao you are spies.

text.NRSV: And he put them all together in prison for three days.
text.Rotherham: So he drew them all together into ward, for three days.
text.KJV: And he put them all together into warde, three dayes.
text.NABRE: With that, he locked them up in the guardhouse for three days.
text.DRB: Therefore he put them in prison three days.

text.NRSV: On the third day Joseph said to them, “Do this and you will live, for I fear God:
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said unto them on the third day, This, do and live,—God himself, do, I, revere.
text.KJV: And Ioseph said vnto them the third day, This doe, and liue: for I feare God.
text.NABRE: On the third day Joseph said to them: “Do this, and you shall live; for I am a God-fearing man.
text.DRB: And the third day bringing them out of prison, he said: Do as I have said, and you shall live: for I fear God.

text.NRSV: if you are honest men, let one of your brothers stay here where you are imprisoned. The rest of you shall go and carry grain for the famine of your households,
text.Rotherham: If ye, are, honest men, one brother of you shall be kept as a prisoner in your house of ward,—but, ye, go, take in corn for the famine of your houses;
text.KJV: If ye be true men, let one of your brethren be bound in the house of your prison: goe ye, carry corne for the famine of your houses.
text.NABRE: If you are honest men, let one of your brothers be confined in this prison, while the rest of you go and take home grain for your starving families.
text.DRB: If you be men of peace, let one of your brethren be bound in prison: and go ye your ways, and carry the corn that you have bought unto your houses,

text.NRSV: and bring your youngest brother to me. Thus your words will be verified, and you shall not die.” And they agreed to do so.
text.Rotherham: and your youngest brother, shall ye bring in unto me, that your words may be confirmed, and ye die not. And they did so.
text.KJV: But bring your yongest brother vnto mee, so shall your wordes be verified, and yee shall not die: and they did so.
text.NABRE: But you must bring me your youngest brother. Your words will thus be verified, and you will not die.” To this they agreed.
text.DRB: and bring your youngest brother to me, that I may prove your sayings to be true, and that you die not. They did as he had said,

text.NRSV: They said to one another, “Alas, we are paying the penalty for what we did to our brother; we saw his anguish when he pleaded with us, but we would not listen. That is why this anguish has come upon us.”
text.Rotherham: And they said each man unto his brother—Verily guilty, we are, respecting our brother, in that we saw the distress of his soul when he appealed unto us for favour and we hearkened not,—therefore, hath come in unto us this distress.
text.KJV: ¶ And they said one to another, We are verily guiltie concerning our brother, in that we saw the anguish of his soule, when he besought vs, and we would not heare: therefore is this distresse come vpon vs.
text.NABRE: To one another, however, they said: “Truly we are being punished because of our brother. We saw the anguish of his heart when he pleaded with us, yet we would not listen. That is why this anguish has now come upon us.”
text.DRB: and they talked one to another: Worthily do we suffer these things, because we have sinned against our brother, seeing the distress of his soul whilst he besought us, and we heard not: therefore is this tribulation come upon us.

text.NRSV: Then Reuben answered them, “Did I not tell you not to wrong the boy? But you would not listen. So now there comes a reckoning for his blood.”
text.Rotherham: And Reuben responded to them, saying—Spake I not unto you saying, Do not sin against the child! And ye hearkened not? His very blood, therefore lo! it is required.
text.KJV: And Reuben answered them, saying, Spake I not vnto you, saying, Doe not sinne against the childe, and ye would not heare? therefore behold also, his blood is required.
text.NABRE: Then Reuben responded, “Did I not tell you, ‘Do no wrong to the boy’? But you would not listen! Now comes the reckoning for his blood.”
text.DRB: Among whom Ruben one of them, said: Why, did not I say to you: Sin not against the boy; and you heard me not? Lo his blood is required.

text.NRSV: They did not know that Joseph understood them, since he spoke with them through an interpreter.
text.Rotherham: But, they, knew not that Joseph was hearkening,—for, the interpreter, was between them.
text.KJV: And they knew not that Ioseph vnderstood them: for hee spake vnto them by an interpreter.
text.NABRE: They did not know, of course, that Joseph understood what they said, since he spoke with them through an interpreter.
text.DRB: And they knew not that Joseph understood: because he spake to them by an interpreter.

text.NRSV: He turned away from them and wept; then he returned and spoke to them. And he picked out Simeon and had him bound before their eyes.
text.Rotherham: So he turned away from them, and wept,—then came back unto them and spake unto them, and took away from them Simeon, and bound him prisoner before their eyes.
text.KJV: And hee turned himselfe about from them and wept, and returned to them againe, and communed with them, and tooke from them Simeon, and bound him before their eyes.
text.NABRE: But turning away from them, he wept. When he was able to speak to them again, he took Simeon from among them and bound him before their eyes.
text.DRB: And he turned away himself a little while, and wept: and returning he spake to them.

text.NRSV: Joseph then gave orders to fill their bags with grain, to return every man’s money to his sack, and to give them provisions for their journey. This was done for them.
text.Rotherham: Then commanded Joseph that their bags should be filled with corn, and their silver be returned each man’s into his sack, and provision be given for the journey,—and it was done to them thus.
text.KJV: ¶ Then Ioseph commanded to fill their sackes with corne, and to restore euery mans money into his sacke, and to giue them prouision for the way: and thus did he vnto them.
text.NABRE: Then Joseph gave orders to have their containers filled with grain, their money replaced in each one’s sack, and provisions given them for their journey. After this had been done for them,
text.DRB: And taking Simeon, and binding him in their presence, he commanded his servants that they should fill up their sacks with wheat, and put every man's money again in their bags, giving them besides for to eat on the way: who did so.

text.NRSV: They loaded their donkeys with their grain, and departed.
text.Rotherham: So they lifted up their corn on their asses,—and went their way, from thence.
text.KJV: And they laded their asses with the corne, and departed thence.
text.NABRE: they loaded their donkeys with the grain and departed.
text.DRB: But they carrying their corn upon their asses, took their journey.

text.NRSV: When one of them opened his sack to give his donkey fodder at the lodging place, he saw his money at the top of the sack.
text.Rotherham: And the first opened his sack to give fodder to his ass at the inn,—and beheld his silver, yea there, it was in the mouth of his sack!
text.KJV: And as one of them opened his sacke, to giue his asse prouender in the Inne, he espied his money: for behold, it was in his sackes mouth.
text.NABRE: At the night encampment, when one of them opened his bag to give his donkey some fodder, he saw his money there in the mouth of his bag.
text.DRB: And one of them opening his sack to give his beast provender in the Inn, beholding the money in the sack's mouth,

text.NRSV: He said to his brothers, “My money has been put back; here it is in my sack!” At this they lost heart and turned trembling to one another, saying, “What is this that God has done to us?”
text.Rotherham: So he said unto his brethren My silver hath been returned, yea verily, there it is in my sack! Then went forth their heart and they turned trembling—each man unto his brother saying. What is this that God hath done to us?
text.KJV: And he said vnto his brethren, My money is restored, and loe, it is euen in my sacke: and their heart failed them, and they were afraid, saying one to an other, What is this that God hath done vnto vs?
text.NABRE: He cried out to his brothers, “My money has been returned! Here it is in my bag!” At that their hearts sank. Trembling, they asked one another, “What is this that God has done to us?”
text.DRB: he said to his brethren: My money is given me again, behold it is in the sack. And being astonished and troubled amongst themselves, they said: What is this, that God hath done unto us?

text.NRSV: When they came to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him all that had happened to them, saying,
text.Rotherham: So they came in unto Jacob their father to the land of Canaan,—and told him all that had befallen them saying:
text.KJV: ¶ And they came vnto Iacob their father, vnto the land of Canaan, and told him all that befell vnto them, saying;
text.NABRE: When they got back to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him all that had happened to them.
text.DRB: And they came to Jacob their father into the land of Chanaan, & they told him all things that had chanced unto them, saying:

text.NRSV: “The man, the lord of the land, spoke harshly to us, and charged us with spying on the land.
text.Rotherham: The man that is lord of the land spake unto us harsh things,—and then delivered us up. as spies of the land.
text.KJV: The man who is the lord of the land, spake roughly to vs, and tooke vs for spies of the countrey.
text.NABRE: “The man who is lord of the land,” they said, “spoke to us harshly and put us in custody on the grounds that we were spying on the land.
text.DRB: The lord of the land spake to us roughly, & thought us to be spies of the province,

text.NRSV: But we said to him, ‘We are honest men, we are not spies.
text.Rotherham: And we said unto him Honest men, are, we,—we are not spies!
text.KJV: And we said vnto him, We are true men; we are no spies.
text.NABRE: But we said to him: ‘We are honest men; we have never been spies.
text.DRB: to whom we answered: We are men of peace, neither do we attempt any treachery.

text.NRSV: We are twelve brothers, sons of our father; one is no more, and the youngest is now with our father in the land of Canaan.’
text.Rotherham: Twelve brethren, are, we, sons of our father,—the one, is not, and the youngest is this day with our father in the land of Canaan.
text.KJV: We be twelue brethren, sonnes of our father: one is not, and the yongest is this day with our father, in the land of Canaan.
text.NABRE: We are twelve brothers, sons of the same father; but one is no more, and the youngest one is now with our father in the land of Canaan.’
text.DRB: We are twelve brethren born of one father: one is not living, the youngest is with our father in the Land of Chanaan.

text.NRSV: Then the man, the lord of the land, said to us, ‘By this I shall know that you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, take grain for the famine of your households, and go your way.
text.Rotherham: Then said the man the lord of the land unto us, Hereby, shall I get to know that honest men, ye are,—One brother of you, leave ye remaining with me, and corn for the famine of your houses, take ye and go your way;
text.KJV: And the man the lord of the countrey said vnto vs, Hereby shall I know that ye are true men: leaue one of your brethren here with me, and take foode for the famine of your housholds, and be gone.
text.NABRE: Then the man who is lord of the land said to us: ‘This is how I will know if you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, then take grain for your starving families and go.
text.DRB: Who said to us: Thus shall I try that you be men of peace: Leave one of your brethren with me, and take ye provision necessary for your houses, and go your ways,

text.NRSV: Bring your youngest brother to me, and I shall know that you are not spies but honest men. Then I will release your brother to you, and you may trade in the land.’ ”
text.Rotherham: And bring ye in your youngest brother unto me, So must I get to know that ye are, not spies, but are honest men: Your brother, will I give up to you, and with the land, shall ye traffic.
text.KJV: And bring your yongest brother vnto me: then shall I know that you are no spies, but that you are true men: so will I deliuer you your brother, and ye shall traffique in the land.
text.NABRE: When you bring me your youngest brother, and I know that you are not spies but honest men, I will restore your brother to you, and you may move about freely in the land.’”
text.DRB: and bring your youngest brother to me, that I may know you are not spies: and you may receive this fellow again, that is kept in prison: and afterwards may have license to buy what things you will.

text.NRSV: As they were emptying their sacks, there in each one’s sack was his bag of money. When they and their father saw their bundles of money, they were dismayed.
